headline: YAOG is close to rc.1 but NOT ready: ship after fixing 6 concrete blockers (panic-recovery gap on the fleet muxes, allocator compile-DoS, enroll identity-takeover + revoke-resurrection, and the refreshed-cookie keystone-signing 401); the local-mode TS-compiler rewrite is post-rc.1 and must not start mid-beta.
rc1_blockers: B1 (VERIFIED): recoverPanics wraps ONLY the air-gap mux; RegisterOperatorRoutes (handler_controller.go:213) and RegisterAgentRoutes (handler_controller.go:195) have NO panic recovery, and ListenAndServeAgent mounts s.agentMux directly (server.go:228-237). A panic in any fleet/agent handler tears the connection instead of returning CodeInternalPanic. Fix: wrap both muxes. Cheapest blocker.

S1 (HIGH): allocator compile-DoS at internal/allocator/ip.go:143 - /8 CIDR + low reserved range pins a CPU core for 2m+ with no context cancellation; anonymous in local/air-gap mode. Fix: thread context.Context + cancel on request-done, cap effective host-space and node*scan product.

S4+S5 (MEDIUM, fix together): enroll unconditionally overwrites a live approved node's WG key+bearer (enrollment.go:188, silent identity hijack) AND revoke does not invalidate outstanding enrollment tokens (handler_controller.go:1137, a still-valid token resurrects a revoked node). Fix: guard hostile same-id re-enroll; invalidate enrollment tokens on revoke + add a NodeRevoked guard in Enroll.

S6 (LOW but cheap): enrollment-token TTL has no server-side cap (handler_controller.go:1358 only checks <=0). Combined with S4/S5 it is a standing year-long node-takeover capability. Fix: one-line server-side max-TTL clamp.

F1 (frontend fleet bug): getTrustlist (controllerClient.ts:971) is the only authed route bypassing the shared request() helper - no credentials:'include', no CSRF - so a refreshed cookie-only operator session gets 401 and CANNOT keystone-sign on Deploy on a keystone-ON tenant. Fix: route it through request().

C1 (HIGH byte-stability): babeld.conf line order tracks edge-array position, not link identity - Pass 2 appends PeerInfo in topo.Edges order (peers.go:570) and babel.go:118-134/167-171 concatenate in slice order, so a benign edge reorder changes an unchanged node's config hash / bundle digest (violates I2/I8). Fix: sort each node's peer slice by InterfaceName before rendering.
post_rc1_roadmap: Local-mode TS compiler rewrite - ONLY after an explicit written WASM-vs-TS tradeoff with the owner (the conformance harness exists solely because TS was chosen). Build internal/conformance/ harness FIRST with fixed-key fixtures, retroactively pin the already-drifting frontend/src/lib/normalizeEdges.ts duplication, define a Go-side coverage FLOOR + structural guards, and make it a permanent required CI gate. Bonus: removes the anonymous air-gap attack surface (S1-S3, B4).

Remaining compile-DoS hardening: S2 (cap topology.Domains and per-domain reserved_ranges, schema.go:76), S3 (peers.go:938 quadratic gap-fill -> free-cursor + cached ParseCIDR + context), B4 (stream the export ZIP instead of buffering the whole archive in a bytes.Buffer).

SSRF completeness/hardening: S7 (unwrap IPv4-compatible ::a.b.c.d, release_pins.go:78), S8 (collapse dial errors to a generic detail, release_pins.go:347), S11 (add OCI 192.0.0.192/32 to the deny set).

Compiler correctness: C2 (auto-heal-on-reenable + targeted test for the disabled->reenable pin collision), C3 (derive/normalize HasPublicIP from len(PublicEndpoints)>0 or warn on mismatch, peers.go:783).

Frontend: add a test runner (vitest) covering the Go-mirroring pure helpers (normalizeEdges/updateStatus/custody/webauthn); add router_id to the TS Node type + NodeEditor input (F2); add an automated guard for the five hand-synced omitempty/pin field lists (F3).

Coverage + hygiene: cover cmd/* mains (two-port boot, SIGTERM drain, agent self-update reconcile call-order), server lifecycle wiring, renameOrCopy; add a doc-vs-code link gate (5 broken spec links, stale repository-structure.md); one isolated PR for the CJK-comment + stub-comment Englishization pass; exclude frontend/node_modules from go test ./....

Decision-required acceptances to record at rc.1: B2 (FileStore fsync durability - fix or document as known limitation alongside the SPOF), B3 (require non-empty passkey login Origin or accept config-gated origin binding + no-counter-check), S9 (per-username login lockout DoS - accept or temper), S10 (document the CSRF double-submit threat boundary: TLS proxy + no untrusted sibling subdomains).

Deferred to rc.2/GA (already acknowledged): FileStore SPOF (global mutex + 200ms poll), bootstrap-TOFU, KMS/OIDC/multi-tenant; the internal/render vs internal/renderer naming rename (cosmetic, do NOT touch mid-beta).
top_risks: R1: The B1 panic-recovery gap is a live fleet-availability hole masked by a doc comment claiming the opposite - a single nil-deref/index panic in a hot agent or operator handler (HandleConfig/HandleEnroll/HandleStage) tears the connection with no coded 5xx, degrading the controller in exactly the fleet-bearing mode rc.1 gates on.

R2: The TS-compiler-rewrite drift risk is structurally unmitigated TODAY (zero conformance fixtures, zero frontend test runner, and frontend/src/lib/normalizeEdges.ts already hand-mirrors linkKey+PIN_FIELDS from peers.go/linkid.go with nothing pinning them equal). Starting the rewrite mid-beta would compound this against the active #109-#135 PR stream; the skeptic's drift_risk_adequately_mitigated=FALSE is correct.

R3: C1 babel line-ordering can silently churn a deployed node's babeld.conf hash / bundle digest on a benign edge-array reorder, eroding the very incremental-deploy byte-stability (I2/I8) that the project's entire pin/reserve/heal apparatus exists to protect.

R4: Enrollment-token authority (S4/S5/S6) is broader than the 'one-shot first-enrollment' mental model - a single leaked or over-broad token is a standing node-takeover AND revoked-node-resurrection capability for its full (uncapped) TTL, redeemable at the unauthenticated /enroll route.

R5: The three compile-DoS findings are anonymous ONLY in local/air-gap mode (operator-gated in controller mode, never fleet-node-reachable). Mis-scoping them risks either delaying rc.1 for a self-DoS or shipping a publicly-exposed local-mode /api/compile that pins a CPU core on a 320KB body.

confirmed.title: Enroll unconditionally overwrites an existing approved node's WG key + bearer token (node-id takeover / silent identity hijack)
confirmed.description: controller.Enroll() builds a brand-new Node{NodeID, WGPublicKey, APITokenHash, Status:NodeApproved, EnrolledAt:now} and calls store.UpsertNode (filestore.go:448 writes nodes/<id>.json with no read-modify-merge), AFTER having burned the enrollment token. There is NO guard against the node-id already being a live, approved node. The only checks in the path are: (1) ConsumeEnrollmentToken validates the token hash, node-id scope and expiry (filestore.go:1202); (2) isReservedNodeID blocks only the operator name (handler_controller.go:185); (3) CheckWGKeyUnique only refuses if the SAME WG key is already approved under a DIFFERENT node-id (enrollment.go:233). None of these stops a second Enroll for an EXISTING node-id from a DIFFERENT WG key. The result: the existing node's WGPublicKey is replaced, a fresh APITokenHash is minted, and IssueNodeAPIToken (filestore.go:1282) self-cleans the prior reverse-index entry, instantly invalidating the legitimate node's bearer token (it now 401s / loses its config). The takeover is gated by possession of a valid node-scoped enrollment token, so this is an authorization-via-token property, not a freely exploitable one — but it means an enrollment token is a full node-takeover capability for the entire TTL, not merely a one-time first-enrollment grant. Combined with the unbounded TTL (see enroll-token-ttl-unbounded), a single leaked/over-broad token lets the holder seize an already-running node's identity. The comment at enrollment.go:161-168 only contemplates benign same-id re-enroll of a reinstalled host; it does not treat hostile re-enroll of a live node as a takeover.

confirmed.title: Revoking a node does not invalidate its outstanding enrollment tokens — a still-valid token resurrects a revoked node via re-enroll
confirmed.description: HandleRevoke flips node.Status to NodeRevoked and clears the bearer (RevokeNodeAPIToken), but does NOT delete or invalidate any enrollment-token records (tokens/<hash>.json) scoped to that node-id. Enroll has no NodeRevoked guard: ConsumeEnrollmentToken only checks hash/node-id/expiry/consumed (filestore.go:1202-1207), then Enroll's UpsertNode (enrollment.go:188-197) writes Status:NodeApproved unconditionally. So an unexpired, unconsumed enrollment token for a node that was just revoked can be redeemed at the unauthenticated /enroll route to re-create the node as approved with a fresh bearer — undoing the revocation without any operator action. The audit trail records an 'enroll' entry (enrollment.go:208-215) so it is tamper-evident, but the eviction is silently reversed.

confirmed.title: Enrollment-token TTL has no upper bound — operator can mint a long-lived token that is a standing node-takeover/resurrection capability
confirmed.description: HandleEnrollmentToken validates only TTLSeconds <= 0 (handler_controller.go:1358); there is no maximum. NewEnrollmentToken sets ExpiresAt = now + ttl with no clamp (enrollment.go:75). A fat-fingered or convenience-driven TTL (e.g. ttl_seconds = 31536000) yields a token valid for a year. Because Enroll overwrites existing nodes and re-approves revoked ones (see enroll-overwrites-live-node-identity and revoke-does-not-invalidate-enrollment-tokens), a long-lived enrollment token is effectively a long-lived bearer of full node-takeover authority for its node-id, not a brief one-shot provisioning grant. The single-use burn limits it to one redemption, but that one redemption can hijack/resurrect at any point in the window. Defense-in-depth would cap TTL server-side (the per-node bearer it issues is the durable credential; the enrollment token only needs a short minting-to-bootstrap window).

confirmed.title: SSRF egress guard is well-engineered and effective against the charter's named bypasses
confirmed.description: The ONLY server-side outbound HTTP egress in the controller is HandleReleasePins via h.releaseClient (verified: grep for http.Get/Post/DefaultClient/.Do/http.Client in internal/ outside agent/ finds only release_pins.go). HandleSettings makes NO outbound request — it only validates+stores URLs that the NODE later fetches via the bootstrap script (and those baked values are shQuote-protected + whitespace-rejected by validateAbsoluteHTTPURL, handler_bootstrap.go:220-238,328-330). The egress defense is the net.Dialer.Control hook blockPrivateAddr (release_pins.go:51), which runs AFTER DNS resolution on EVERY connection — including each redirect hop, since Go re-dials redirects through the same transport — so it structurally defeats DNS-rebinding, the class a URL-format check cannot stop. isPublicUnicastIP (release_pins.go:70) correctly rejects: loopback, link-local unicast (covers AWS/GCP IMDS 169.254.169.254), link-local/interface-local/all multicast, unspecified (0.0.0.0 / ::), RFC1918+ULA (IsPrivate), and RFC6598 CGNAT 100.64/10 (covers Alibaba metadata 100.100.100.200) — all confirmed public=false by direct execution of the exact code. It additionally unwraps 6to4 (2002::/16) and NAT64 well-known (64:ff9b::/96) embedded IPv4 and re-checks it, so a 6to4/NAT64 form of 127.0.0.1 or 169.254.169.254 is also blocked (confirmed). Scheme abuse (file://, gopher://) is blocked by validateAbsoluteHTTPURL (scheme must be http/https) and by CheckRedirect rejecting non-http(s) redirect hops (release_pins.go:133-135); env proxies are disabled (Proxy: nil); redirects capped at 5; body capped at 512 bytes and validated to a 64-hex SHA-256 (fetchSidecar:353-360), near-eliminating blind exfiltration. Operator-only gating is airtight: release-pins is wrapped op = cors(operatorAuth) (handler_controller.go:262), the cookie path requires double-submit CSRF on the state-changing POST (auth_controller.go:162), the Bearer path cannot be set cross-site, and there is no lower-trust operator tier (operatorAuth is binary). This is a strong, evidence-backed sound result; the remaining findings are low-residual gaps, not breaks.

confirmed.title: isPublicUnicastIP treats IPv4-compatible IPv6 (::a.b.c.d, e.g. ::127.0.0.1) as public
confirmed.description: isPublicUnicastIP unwraps only IPv4-MAPPED (::ffff:a.b.c.d, via net.IP.To4) and 6to4/NAT64-embedded IPv4 (embeddedIPv4), but NOT the deprecated IPv4-COMPATIBLE form ::a.b.c.d (RFC 4291 sec 2.5.5.1). Verified by running the exact code: net.ParseIP("::127.0.0.1").To4() returns nil and IsLoopback() returns false, so isPublicUnicastIP(::127.0.0.1) returns true (public) and blockPrivateAddr would permit the dial. The same holds for ::169.254.169.254 etc. This is the one address family the layered guard does not normalize. Practical exploitability is LOW: the IPv4-compatible format is formally deprecated and modern Linux/BSD kernels do NOT route ::127.0.0.1 to the loopback interface (it is not a loopback-mapped address there), and an attacker would additionally need a DNS resolver to return an AAAA of this form during a rebind. So it is a defense-in-depth completeness gap, not a demonstrated live loopback reach. Fix is one line: also call embeddedIPv4-style unwrap for the ::a.b.c.d / ::ffff:0:a.b.c.d (IPv4-translatable 64:ff9b:1::/48 is already partly out of scope) prefixes, or reject any IPv6 whose low 32 bits decode to a non-public IPv4 when the high 96 bits are zero.

confirmed.title: Dial-guard rejection reflects the resolved internal hostname/IP back to the client (DNS-resolution oracle)
confirmed.description: fetchSidecar maps any transport error to apierr.CodeAgentReleaseFetchFailed with .With("detail", err.Error()) (release_pins.go:347), and that error string, for a blockPrivateAddr rejection, is the verbatim guard message 'release-pin dial: refusing to connect to non-public address <IP>' (release_pins.go:61) carrying the RESOLVED IP. The apierr template for that code is 'Could not fetch the release checksum from {url}: {detail}' (apierr.go:197), and writeAPIError serializes both Message and Params (which include detail) into the JSON error body (handler.go:594-595, errorBody at handler.go:52-56). Confirmed end-to-end: the resolved private IP and the differential outcome (guard-reject vs connection-refused vs timeout vs 502-status) are returned to the caller. This turns the endpoint into an internal DNS-resolution / private-IP-existence oracle: by submitting an internal hostname as base, the caller learns whether it resolves to a private/loopback/metadata IP and what that IP is, even though the actual fetch is blocked. Severity is LOW because the endpoint is operator-only (operatorAuth) — the disclosure is to an already-trusted actor, not an anonymous attacker, and the SSRF itself is blocked; the residual risk is recon leverage if an operator session token is compromised. Mitigation: collapse all transport/dial failures to a generic 'fetch failed' detail (do not echo err.Error() / the resolved IP) for this endpoint.

confirmed.title: Oracle OCI metadata endpoint 192.0.0.192 is treated as public by the egress guard
confirmed.description: isPublicUnicastIP returns public=true for 192.0.0.192 (confirmed by direct execution). 192.0.0.0/24 is IANA 'IETF Protocol Assignments' space and is not flagged by any of Go's IsPrivate/IsLinkLocal* predicates, but 192.0.0.192 is Oracle Cloud Infrastructure's instance-metadata address. The other major-cloud metadata addresses ARE blocked (AWS/GCP 169.254.169.254 link-local; Alibaba 100.100.100.200 CGNAT; AWS IMDSv6 fd00:ec2::254 ULA — all confirmed public=false), so this is a single-cloud completeness gap rather than a systemic miss. Practical impact is minimal here: even if reached, the response body is read through a 512-byte LimitReader and rejected unless its first whitespace token is a 64-hex SHA-256 (fetchSidecar:353-360), so OCI metadata JSON cannot be exfiltrated via this endpoint — at most it is an existence/timing signal, and only to an authenticated operator. Recorded as info / hardening: add 192.0.0.192/32 (and optionally the broader special-purpose 192.0.0.0/24) to the deny set if OCI deployments are in scope.

confirmed.title: IP allocator scans the full domain host-space per node; a /8 CIDR + low reserved range pins a CPU core for minutes (unauthenticated, 320 KB body)
confirmed.description: allocateFromCIDR linearly scans candidate addresses from network+1 to the end of the domain CIDR, and for EACH candidate allocates a net.IP, calls uint32ToIP().String(), does map lookups, and iterates every reserved net calling rNet.Contains(). The schema only requires the domain CIDR prefix be >= /8 (schema.go:160-163), so a /8 (16.7M hosts) is accepted, and there is NO cap on the number of nodes' worth of allocation OR on reserved_ranges. AllocateIPs (ip.go:62-82) calls this once per node and the inner loop ALWAYS restarts from startHost, so the cost is roughly nodes * scan-distance. I measured the exact /api/compile path: a 320 KB JSON body with 2000 nodes (the max), domain CIDR 10.0.0.0/8, and a single reserved range 10.0.0.0/12 (~1M low addresses) ran compiler.Compile() for 2m0s of pure CPU and still returned successfully (err=nil). Widening the reserved block to /9 (~8.4M scan/node) ran past 600s (effectively unbounded for the attacker). The loop takes no context.Context, so a client disconnect or the server's 120s WriteTimeout (server.go:210-213, a connection deadline) does NOT abort the in-progress goroutine. recoverPanics (server.go:161) only catches panics, not CPU/memory blowup, exactly as the charter notes.

confirmed.title: No cap on number of domains or reserved_ranges entries; multiplies the allocator/validator cost beyond the node/edge bounds
confirmed.description: topologyExceedsBounds only bounds Nodes (<=2000) and Edges (<=10000) and the alloc schema version. Topology.Domains (model.go:19) and Domain.ReservedRanges (model.go:53) are unbounded slices that count against the 4 MiB body cap only. validateDomainsSchema (schema.go:135-229) and the allocator both iterate reserved_ranges per domain, and allocateFromCIDR (ip.go:160-165) iterates ALL parsed reservedNets for every candidate IP scanned. A body filled with tens of thousands of reserved CIDR strings (each parsed by net.ParseCIDR repeatedly) combined with the large-CIDR scan in the previous finding compounds the per-candidate cost. Because the node/edge ceilings were explicitly added as a DoS guard (schema.go:60-68) but the domain/reserved-range dimension was left open, the guard is incomplete for the compile-time-exhaustion surface.

confirmed.title: Peer-derivation gap-fill rescans transit/link-local pools from index 0 on every link; many same-pair backup edges with a large transit_cidr give O(links^2) CPU
confirmed.description: gapFillTransitPair loops index from 0 to poolPairs calling allocateTransitPair (which net.ParseCIDR()'s the transit CIDR every iteration) and skipping already-used pairs, restarting from 0 for each link; gapFillLinkLocalPair (peers.go:957) is an unbounded for-loop with the same restart-from-0 pattern; lowestFreePort (peers.go:970) similarly scans from base each call. Each role=="backup" edge between the same node pair becomes an independent link (linkid.LinkKey adds #edgeID, linkid.go:53-58), so N backups all on one pair drive N gap-fill calls each rescanning the growing used-set => O(N^2). The default /24 transit pool would short-circuit at 127 pairs (CodeTransitPoolExhausted), but a domain transit_cidr of /12 (~500k pairs, accepted by schema.go:214-227 which only rejects <8 or >30) removes that natural ceiling and lets the quadratic run. Measured: 8000 backup edges between one node pair with transit_cidr 172.16.0.0/12 took ~11s of CPU in DerivePeers; 8000 edges is within the 10000-edge cap and the effective port range (51820+8000) stays < 65535 so it passes validateEffectivePortRanges. Smaller in magnitude than the allocator finding but a second independent compile-time amplifier with no context cancellation.

confirmed.title: Per-username login rate-limiter enables targeted operator lockout DoS
confirmed.description: registerAttempt(now, userKey, ipKey) reserves a slot on BOTH the username key and the IP key, and a key is blocked once count >= maxLoginFailures (10) for the loginWindow (15min). Because the username key is attacker-controllable (req.Username, handler_login.go:77 userKey := "user:" + req.Username) and counts on every FAILED attempt, an unauthenticated attacker who knows/guesses a valid operator username can send 10 wrong-password POST /login requests and lock that operator out of ALL login paths (password, TOTP finish, passkey begin/finish all share h.loginLimiter — handler_passkey.go:340,389) for 15 minutes, repeatable indefinitely. This is an availability issue, not an auth bypass, and is an inherent property of any account-lockout scheme; the file's own header comment acknowledges per-username limiting is intended to stop credential stuffing. Flagged because the lockout spans every operator login method, so a single attacker can keep the panel operator perpetually locked out with ~1 request/90s. Mitigations exist (the break-glass YAOG_CONTROLLER_OPERATOR_TOKEN bypasses /login entirely), so impact is bounded.

confirmed.title: Double-submit CSRF token is not server-side bound to the session (standard double-submit caveat)
confirmed.description: csrfValid only checks that the X-CSRF-Token header equals the yaog_csrf cookie value (constant-time). The CSRF token is a fresh 256-bit CSPRNG value (newCSRFToken, line 121) set as a sibling cookie at login, but the server never records or verifies a binding between a specific csrf value and the specific session it was minted with — any (cookie, header) pair that matches each other passes, regardless of which session minted the csrf cookie. For the intended single-controller, exact-origin-allowlist deployment this is safe: an attacker cannot set/read the yaog_csrf cookie cross-origin (it is same-site/None+Secure scoped, and credentialed CORS is restricted to SetPanelOrigins). The residual weakness is the textbook double-submit limitation: a same-registrable-domain sibling host (or any MITM able to write a cookie for the controller's domain over a non-Secure path when YAOG_SECURE_COOKIE=false) could plant a known csrf cookie + matching header and ride the victim's ambient yaog_session cookie. No concrete in-repo exploit; recorded so the threat boundary (TLS-terminating proxy + no untrusted sibling subdomains) is explicit rather than assumed. The Bearer-token path is unaffected (CSRF-exempt by design, auth_controller.go:152-155).

confirmed.title: Operator credential RPID/Origin are unvalidated and expand unquoted into the bootstrap systemd ExecStart / one-shot run command
confirmed.description: handleOperatorCredentialPin (handler_controller.go:1659) accepts RPID and Origin from the operator JSON and stores them verbatim: only Alg is checked against a fixed enum and PublicKeyPEM is parsed; RPID/Origin get NO character-class validation (unlike the mimic catalog at handler_bootstrap.go:270, which explicitly rejects shell-dangerous bytes). Those raw values are baked into the bootstrap script. renderBootstrapScript (handler_bootstrap.go:348-350) shQuote-s them at the *assignment* (OPERATOR_RPID=... is safe), but the script body then expands them UNQUOTED at handler_bootstrap.go:445-446: `OP_FLAGS="$OP_FLAGS --operator-rpid ${OPERATOR_RPID}"`, and OP_FLAGS is in turn expanded UNQUOTED into the systemd ExecStart line (line 466) and the --once invocation (line 488). Because bash does NOT re-interpret command substitution / `;` / `|` from a variable's *value* (only word-splitting + globbing), this is argument injection, not command execution: a value containing spaces splits into extra flags/arguments passed to `yaog-agent run`. It is NOT remote-attacker reachable — POST /operator-credential is operator-auth-gated, so the only actor who can set a malicious RPID/Origin is the already-trusted operator configuring their own fleet (self-inflicted). Severity is low for that reason, but it is a real input-validation gap: the field should be validated at pin time (like the mimic-catalog fields) or the script body should double-quote ${OP_FLAGS}.

confirmed.title: Without a pinned bundle key AND without keystone, VerifyBundle trusts the bundle's own signing-pubkey.pem (plain-HTTP MITM can swap both)
confirmed.description: VerifyBundle (verify.go:146) chooses the verification key as: pinned key wins, otherwise the bundle's OWN signing-pubkey.pem (verify.go:170-176, trust-on-first-supply). So in the configuration where (a) no operator --pubkey is pinned, (b) no off-host operator credential is configured (keystone OFF, so VerifyMembership is a no-op at verify.go:297), and (c) transport is plain HTTP with no reverse-proxy TLS (the documented by-design posture, enrollment.go:21-22), an active network attacker can replace the served bundle AND its bundled signing-pubkey.pem + bundle.sig with a self-consistent malicious set; VerifyBundle then verifies against the attacker-supplied key and res.Signed=true, and install.sh runs as root on the node. This is the documented bootstrap-TOFU hole, and it is correctly defended by the keystone (the off-host operator credential is the real anchor and is NOT bundle-shipped, verify.go:246), so a fleet that pins an operator credential is safe even over plain HTTP. The residual exposure is the keystone-OFF + no-pubkey + no-TLS combination, which is a back-compat default rather than the recommended controller posture. The code comments (verify.go:135-140) acknowledge this; reporting it so the first-RC release notes make the TLS-or-keystone requirement non-optional for production.

confirmed_full.attack_path: Attacker obtains a valid, unexpired enrollment token scoped to an already-enrolled node-id 'alpha' (e.g. an operator re-minted a token for alpha, a token was leaked, or a long-TTL token from initial provisioning was never consumed/expired). Attacker POSTs /api/v1/agent/enroll {enrollment_token, node_id:'alpha', wg_public_key:<attacker key>}. Enroll burns the token, then UpsertNode overwrites alpha's record with the attacker's WG public key and a fresh bearer token returned to the attacker. The legitimate alpha node's old bearer stops resolving; the attacker now receives alpha's config bundle on /config and is the trusted peer for alpha's overlay IP (subject to the keystone trust-list still pinning alpha's bundle SHA, which constrains config tampering but not the identity/token seizure itself).
