confirmed_full.attack_path: Operator revokes node 'beta' (compromised host). A valid enrollment token for 'beta' still exists (e.g. minted with a generous TTL during provisioning, or the operator minted-then-revoked in the wrong order). The attacker (or the still-compromised host) POSTs /enroll with that token and any WG key; Enroll burns the token and UpsertNode resets beta to NodeApproved with a new bearer. Beta is back in the fleet and is served config on the next promote, defeating the revocation. The window is the token's remaining TTL, which is operator-chosen and unbounded (see enroll-token-ttl-unbounded).

confirmed_full.title: Enrollment-token TTL has no upper bound — operator can mint a long-lived token that is a standing node-takeover/resurrection capability
confirmed_full.description: HandleEnrollmentToken validates only TTLSeconds <= 0 (handler_controller.go:1358); there is no maximum. NewEnrollmentToken sets ExpiresAt = now + ttl with no clamp (enrollment.go:75). A fat-fingered or convenience-driven TTL (e.g. ttl_seconds = 31536000) yields a token valid for a year. Because Enroll overwrites existing nodes and re-approves revoked ones (see enroll-overwrites-live-node-identity and revoke-does-not-invalidate-enrollment-tokens), a long-lived enrollment token is effectively a long-lived bearer of full node-takeover authority for its node-id, not a brief one-shot provisioning grant. The single-use burn limits it to one redemption, but that one redemption can hijack/resurrect at any point in the window. Defense-in-depth would cap TTL server-side (the per-node bearer it issues is the durable credential; the enrollment token only needs a short minting-to-bootstrap window).
confirmed_full.attack_path: Operator mints an enrollment token with a very long TTL (UI convenience or to pre-provision a batch of nodes). The plaintext token sits in provisioning notes / a config-management secret store / chat scrollback for months. Anyone who later reads it can redeem it once against /enroll to seize or resurrect that node-id at any time before the year elapses, with no further operator interaction. A server-side TTL cap (e.g. <= a few hours) would shrink this window to the legitimate bootstrap duration.

confirmed_full.title: SSRF egress guard is well-engineered and effective against the charter's named bypasses
confirmed_full.description: The ONLY server-side outbound HTTP egress in the controller is HandleReleasePins via h.releaseClient (verified: grep for http.Get/Post/DefaultClient/.Do/http.Client in internal/ outside agent/ finds only release_pins.go). HandleSettings makes NO outbound request — it only validates+stores URLs that the NODE later fetches via the bootstrap script (and those baked values are shQuote-protected + whitespace-rejected by validateAbsoluteHTTPURL, handler_bootstrap.go:220-238,328-330). The egress defense is the net.Dialer.Control hook blockPrivateAddr (release_pins.go:51), which runs AFTER DNS resolution on EVERY connection — including each redirect hop, since Go re-dials redirects through the same transport — so it structurally defeats DNS-rebinding, the class a URL-format check cannot stop. isPublicUnicastIP (release_pins.go:70) correctly rejects: loopback, link-local unicast (covers AWS/GCP IMDS 169.254.169.254), link-local/interface-local/all multicast, unspecified (0.0.0.0 / ::), RFC1918+ULA (IsPrivate), and RFC6598 CGNAT 100.64/10 (covers Alibaba metadata 100.100.100.200) — all confirmed public=false by direct execution of the exact code. It additionally unwraps 6to4 (2002::/16) and NAT64 well-known (64:ff9b::/96) embedded IPv4 and re-checks it, so a 6to4/NAT64 form of 127.0.0.1 or 169.254.169.254 is also blocked (confirmed). Scheme abuse (file://, gopher://) is blocked by validateAbsoluteHTTPURL (scheme must be http/https) and by CheckRedirect rejecting non-http(s) redirect hops (release_pins.go:133-135); env proxies are disabled (Proxy: nil); redirects capped at 5; body capped at 512 bytes and validated to a 64-hex SHA-256 (fetchSidecar:353-360), near-eliminating blind exfiltration. Operator-only gating is airtight: release-pins is wrapped op = cors(operatorAuth) (handler_controller.go:262), the cookie path requires double-submit CSRF on the state-changing POST (auth_controller.go:162), the Bearer path cannot be set cross-site, and there is no lower-trust operator tier (operatorAuth is binary). This is a strong, evidence-backed sound result; the remaining findings are low-residual gaps, not breaks.
confirmed_full.attack_path: n/a — this entry records that the primary SSRF defenses hold against DNS-rebinding, redirect-to-internal, cloud-metadata (169.254.169.254), CGNAT metadata, IPv6 embedded-IPv4, scheme abuse, and CSRF/lower-trust reach.

confirmed_full.title: isPublicUnicastIP treats IPv4-compatible IPv6 (::a.b.c.d, e.g. ::127.0.0.1) as public
confirmed_full.description: isPublicUnicastIP unwraps only IPv4-MAPPED (::ffff:a.b.c.d, via net.IP.To4) and 6to4/NAT64-embedded IPv4 (embeddedIPv4), but NOT the deprecated IPv4-COMPATIBLE form ::a.b.c.d (RFC 4291 sec 2.5.5.1). Verified by running the exact code: net.ParseIP("::127.0.0.1").To4() returns nil and IsLoopback() returns false, so isPublicUnicastIP(::127.0.0.1) returns true (public) and blockPrivateAddr would permit the dial. The same holds for ::169.254.169.254 etc. This is the one address family the layered guard does not normalize. Practical exploitability is LOW: the IPv4-compatible format is formally deprecated and modern Linux/BSD kernels do NOT route ::127.0.0.1 to the loopback interface (it is not a loopback-mapped address there), and an attacker would additionally need a DNS resolver to return an AAAA of this form during a rebind. So it is a defense-in-depth completeness gap, not a demonstrated live loopback reach. Fix is one line: also call embeddedIPv4-style unwrap for the ::a.b.c.d / ::ffff:0:a.b.c.d (IPv4-translatable 64:ff9b:1::/48 is already partly out of scope) prefixes, or reject any IPv6 whose low 32 bits decode to a non-public IPv4 when the high 96 bits are zero.
confirmed_full.attack_path: Operator-authenticated POST /api/v1/operator/release-pins with base set to a host whose DNS AAAA record resolves to ::127.0.0.1 (or ::169.254.169.254); blockPrivateAddr permits the dial because To4()=nil and IsLoopback()=false. On a host/kernel that routes IPv4-compatible IPv6 to the embedded IPv4 (uncommon, mostly historical), this would reach an internal service. The 512-byte sha256-validated body still constrains exfiltration to existence/timing.

confirmed_full.title: Dial-guard rejection reflects the resolved internal hostname/IP back to the client (DNS-resolution oracle)
confirmed_full.description: fetchSidecar maps any transport error to apierr.CodeAgentReleaseFetchFailed with .With("detail", err.Error()) (release_pins.go:347), and that error string, for a blockPrivateAddr rejection, is the verbatim guard message 'release-pin dial: refusing to connect to non-public address <IP>' (release_pins.go:61) carrying the RESOLVED IP. The apierr template for that code is 'Could not fetch the release checksum from {url}: {detail}' (apierr.go:197), and writeAPIError serializes both Message and Params (which include detail) into the JSON error body (handler.go:594-595, errorBody at handler.go:52-56). Confirmed end-to-end: the resolved private IP and the differential outcome (guard-reject vs connection-refused vs timeout vs 502-status) are returned to the caller. This turns the endpoint into an internal DNS-resolution / private-IP-existence oracle: by submitting an internal hostname as base, the caller learns whether it resolves to a private/loopback/metadata IP and what that IP is, even though the actual fetch is blocked. Severity is LOW because the endpoint is operator-only (operatorAuth) — the disclosure is to an already-trusted actor, not an anonymous attacker, and the SSRF itself is blocked; the residual risk is recon leverage if an operator session token is compromised. Mitigation: collapse all transport/dial failures to a generic 'fetch failed' detail (do not echo err.Error() / the resolved IP) for this endpoint.
confirmed_full.attack_path: Operator (or holder of a stolen operator session/break-glass token, e.g. via XSS or token leak) POSTs /release-pins with base pointing at candidate internal hostnames; the returned error message/params reveal each hostname's resolved IP and whether it is private/loopback/metadata, enabling internal-network mapping despite the dial being blocked.

confirmed_full.title: Oracle OCI metadata endpoint 192.0.0.192 is treated as public by the egress guard
confirmed_full.description: isPublicUnicastIP returns public=true for 192.0.0.192 (confirmed by direct execution). 192.0.0.0/24 is IANA 'IETF Protocol Assignments' space and is not flagged by any of Go's IsPrivate/IsLinkLocal* predicates, but 192.0.0.192 is Oracle Cloud Infrastructure's instance-metadata address. The other major-cloud metadata addresses ARE blocked (AWS/GCP 169.254.169.254 link-local; Alibaba 100.100.100.200 CGNAT; AWS IMDSv6 fd00:ec2::254 ULA — all confirmed public=false), so this is a single-cloud completeness gap rather than a systemic miss. Practical impact is minimal here: even if reached, the response body is read through a 512-byte LimitReader and rejected unless its first whitespace token is a 64-hex SHA-256 (fetchSidecar:353-360), so OCI metadata JSON cannot be exfiltrated via this endpoint — at most it is an existence/timing signal, and only to an authenticated operator. Recorded as info / hardening: add 192.0.0.192/32 (and optionally the broader special-purpose 192.0.0.0/24) to the deny set if OCI deployments are in scope.
confirmed_full.attack_path: On an Oracle Cloud host, an authenticated operator POSTs /release-pins with base resolving to 192.0.0.192; the dial is permitted. The sha256-hex body validator prevents exfiltrating the metadata document, limiting the result to a reachability/timing oracle.

confirmed_full.title: IP allocator scans the full domain host-space per node; a /8 CIDR + low reserved range pins a CPU core for minutes (unauthenticated, 320 KB body)
confirmed_full.description: allocateFromCIDR linearly scans candidate addresses from network+1 to the end of the domain CIDR, and for EACH candidate allocates a net.IP, calls uint32ToIP().String(), does map lookups, and iterates every reserved net calling rNet.Contains(). The schema only requires the domain CIDR prefix be >= /8 (schema.go:160-163), so a /8 (16.7M hosts) is accepted, and there is NO cap on the number of nodes' worth of allocation OR on reserved_ranges. AllocateIPs (ip.go:62-82) calls this once per node and the inner loop ALWAYS restarts from startHost, so the cost is roughly nodes * scan-distance. I measured the exact /api/compile path: a 320 KB JSON body with 2000 nodes (the max), domain CIDR 10.0.0.0/8, and a single reserved range 10.0.0.0/12 (~1M low addresses) ran compiler.Compile() for 2m0s of pure CPU and still returned successfully (err=nil). Widening the reserved block to /9 (~8.4M scan/node) ran past 600s (effectively unbounded for the attacker). The loop takes no context.Context, so a client disconnect or the server's 120s WriteTimeout (server.go:210-213, a connection deadline) does NOT abort the in-progress goroutine. recoverPanics (server.go:161) only catches panics, not CPU/memory blowup, exactly as the charter notes.
confirmed_full.attack_path: Attacker reaches the operator port. In LOCAL mode /api/compile, /api/export, /api/deploy-script are UNAUTHENTICATED (server.go:117-120 gateAirgap passthrough). POST a ~320 KB topology: one domain {cidr:"10.0.0.0/8", reserved_ranges:["10.0.0.0/9"]} plus 2000 trivial peer nodes referencing it. The request passes schema+semantic validation (well under the 4 MiB body cap and the 2000-node bound) and enters Pass 3 IP allocation, where each node forces a multi-million-address scan. One request pins a CPU core for minutes-to-effectively-unbounded; a handful of concurrent requests exhausts all cores and starves the server. In CONTROLLER mode the same compile pipeline runs (controller/compile.go CompileSubgraph -> compiler.Compile) so an authenticated operator (or anything that can reach the air-gap routes if mis-deployed) has the same primitive.

confirmed_full.title: No cap on number of domains or reserved_ranges entries; multiplies the allocator/validator cost beyond the node/edge bounds
confirmed_full.description: topologyExceedsBounds only bounds Nodes (<=2000) and Edges (<=10000) and the alloc schema version. Topology.Domains (model.go:19) and Domain.ReservedRanges (model.go:53) are unbounded slices that count against the 4 MiB body cap only. validateDomainsSchema (schema.go:135-229) and the allocator both iterate reserved_ranges per domain, and allocateFromCIDR (ip.go:160-165) iterates ALL parsed reservedNets for every candidate IP scanned. A body filled with tens of thousands of reserved CIDR strings (each parsed by net.ParseCIDR repeatedly) combined with the large-CIDR scan in the previous finding compounds the per-candidate cost. Because the node/edge ceilings were explicitly added as a DoS guard (schema.go:60-68) but the domain/reserved-range dimension was left open, the guard is incomplete for the compile-time-exhaustion surface.
confirmed_full.attack_path: Same unauthenticated air-gap POST as the primary finding, but the body instead packs one /8 domain with thousands of reserved_ranges entries (e.g. 30000 distinct /24 CIDRs, ~hundreds of KB) so that every candidate IP scanned in allocateFromCIDR walks the full reservedNets list, multiplying the inner-loop constant factor on top of the already-large scan distance.

confirmed_full.title: Peer-derivation gap-fill rescans transit/link-local pools from index 0 on every link; many same-pair backup edges with a large transit_cidr give O(links^2) CPU
confirmed_full.description: gapFillTransitPair loops index from 0 to poolPairs calling allocateTransitPair (which net.ParseCIDR()'s the transit CIDR every iteration) and skipping already-used pairs, restarting from 0 for each link; gapFillLinkLocalPair (peers.go:957) is an unbounded for-loop with the same restart-from-0 pattern; lowestFreePort (peers.go:970) similarly scans from base each call. Each role=="backup" edge between the same node pair becomes an independent link (linkid.LinkKey adds #edgeID, linkid.go:53-58), so N backups all on one pair drive N gap-fill calls each rescanning the growing used-set => O(N^2). The default /24 transit pool would short-circuit at 127 pairs (CodeTransitPoolExhausted), but a domain transit_cidr of /12 (~500k pairs, accepted by schema.go:214-227 which only rejects <8 or >30) removes that natural ceiling and lets the quadratic run. Measured: 8000 backup edges between one node pair with transit_cidr 172.16.0.0/12 took ~11s of CPU in DerivePeers; 8000 edges is within the 10000-edge cap and the effective port range (51820+8000) stays < 65535 so it passes validateEffectivePortRanges. Smaller in magnitude than the allocator finding but a second independent compile-time amplifier with no context cancellation.
confirmed_full.attack_path: Unauthenticated air-gap POST (or authenticated controller compile) of a topology with 2 router nodes, one domain whose transit_cidr is a /12, and ~8000 edges all FromNodeID=a ToNodeID=b with role="backup". Passes schema+semantic validation and enters peers.go gap-fill, where the per-link pool rescans accumulate to O(edges^2) CPU; combine with the allocator finding for a larger single-request cost.

confirmed_full.title: Per-username login rate-limiter enables targeted operator lockout DoS
confirmed_full.description: registerAttempt(now, userKey, ipKey) reserves a slot on BOTH the username key and the IP key, and a key is blocked once count >= maxLoginFailures (10) for the loginWindow (15min). Because the username key is attacker-controllable (req.Username, handler_login.go:77 userKey := "user:" + req.Username) and counts on every FAILED attempt, an unauthenticated attacker who knows/guesses a valid operator username can send 10 wrong-password POST /login requests and lock that operator out of ALL login paths (password, TOTP finish, passkey begin/finish all share h.loginLimiter — handler_passkey.go:340,389) for 15 minutes, repeatable indefinitely. This is an availability issue, not an auth bypass, and is an inherent property of any account-lockout scheme; the file's own header comment acknowledges per-username limiting is intended to stop credential stuffing. Flagged because the lockout spans every operator login method, so a single attacker can keep the panel operator perpetually locked out with ~1 request/90s. Mitigations exist (the break-glass YAOG_CONTROLLER_OPERATOR_TOKEN bypasses /login entirely), so impact is bounded.
confirmed_full.attack_path: Attacker reaches the operator port (or it is internet-exposed via the reverse proxy). Attacker POSTs /api/v1/operator/login with {username: <known operator>, password: <garbage>} 10 times within 15min; registerAttempt tips the user: key to maxLoginFailures and returns allowed=false (429). The legitimate operator's correct-password login now also hits the locked user: key and is rejected with 429 until the window elapses. Repeating ~1 req/90s sustains the lockout indefinitely.

confirmed_full.title: Double-submit CSRF token is not server-side bound to the session (standard double-submit caveat)
confirmed_full.description: csrfValid only checks that the X-CSRF-Token header equals the yaog_csrf cookie value (constant-time). The CSRF token is a fresh 256-bit CSPRNG value (newCSRFToken, line 121) set as a sibling cookie at login, but the server never records or verifies a binding between a specific csrf value and the specific session it was minted with — any (cookie, header) pair that matches each other passes, regardless of which session minted the csrf cookie. For the intended single-controller, exact-origin-allowlist deployment this is safe: an attacker cannot set/read the yaog_csrf cookie cross-origin (it is same-site/None+Secure scoped, and credentialed CORS is restricted to SetPanelOrigins). The residual weakness is the textbook double-submit limitation: a same-registrable-domain sibling host (or any MITM able to write a cookie for the controller's domain over a non-Secure path when YAOG_SECURE_COOKIE=false) could plant a known csrf cookie + matching header and ride the victim's ambient yaog_session cookie. No concrete in-repo exploit; recorded so the threat boundary (TLS-terminating proxy + no untrusted sibling subdomains) is explicit rather than assumed. The Bearer-token path is unaffected (CSRF-exempt by design, auth_controller.go:152-155).
confirmed_full.attack_path: Pre-conditions outside this code (attacker controls a cookie-writable sibling subdomain of the controller host, or YAOG_SECURE_COOKIE=false plus a network position to inject a Set-Cookie over plain HTTP). Attacker writes yaog_csrf=KNOWN for the controller domain; victim's browser still attaches the httpOnly yaog_session ambiently; a cross-site form/fetch from the attacker page that also sends X-CSRF-Token: KNOWN satisfies csrfValid and the request executes as the victim operator. Not reachable in the documented same-origin / exact-allowlist + proxy-TLS deployment.

confirmed_full.title: Operator credential RPID/Origin are unvalidated and expand unquoted into the bootstrap systemd ExecStart / one-shot run command
confirmed_full.description: handleOperatorCredentialPin (handler_controller.go:1659) accepts RPID and Origin from the operator JSON and stores them verbatim: only Alg is checked against a fixed enum and PublicKeyPEM is parsed; RPID/Origin get NO character-class validation (unlike the mimic catalog at handler_bootstrap.go:270, which explicitly rejects shell-dangerous bytes). Those raw values are baked into the bootstrap script. renderBootstrapScript (handler_bootstrap.go:348-350) shQuote-s them at the *assignment* (OPERATOR_RPID=... is safe), but the script body then expands them UNQUOTED at handler_bootstrap.go:445-446: `OP_FLAGS="$OP_FLAGS --operator-rpid ${OPERATOR_RPID}"`, and OP_FLAGS is in turn expanded UNQUOTED into the systemd ExecStart line (line 466) and the --once invocation (line 488). Because bash does NOT re-interpret command substitution / `;` / `|` from a variable's *value* (only word-splitting + globbing), this is argument injection, not command execution: a value containing spaces splits into extra flags/arguments passed to `yaog-agent run`. It is NOT remote-attacker reachable — POST /operator-credential is operator-auth-gated, so the only actor who can set a malicious RPID/Origin is the already-trusted operator configuring their own fleet (self-inflicted). Severity is low for that reason, but it is a real input-validation gap: the field should be validated at pin time (like the mimic-catalog fields) or the script body should double-quote ${OP_FLAGS}.
confirmed_full.attack_path: An authenticated operator (or a compromised operator session) POSTs /api/v1/operator/operator-credential with rpid="example.com --extra-flag value". The value is stored unvalidated, then baked into the unauth GET /bootstrap script. When a node runs the bootstrap, ${OPERATOR_RPID} word-splits into OP_FLAGS, which expands unquoted into the systemd ExecStart, injecting extra arguments into the agent invocation on the node. No cross-tenant or unauthenticated path; bounded to argument-splitting, not shell command execution.

confirmed_full.title: Without a pinned bundle key AND without keystone, VerifyBundle trusts the bundle's own signing-pubkey.pem (plain-HTTP MITM can swap both)
confirmed_full.description: VerifyBundle (verify.go:146) chooses the verification key as: pinned key wins, otherwise the bundle's OWN signing-pubkey.pem (verify.go:170-176, trust-on-first-supply). So in the configuration where (a) no operator --pubkey is pinned, (b) no off-host operator credential is configured (keystone OFF, so VerifyMembership is a no-op at verify.go:297), and (c) transport is plain HTTP with no reverse-proxy TLS (the documented by-design posture, enrollment.go:21-22), an active network attacker can replace the served bundle AND its bundled signing-pubkey.pem + bundle.sig with a self-consistent malicious set; VerifyBundle then verifies against the attacker-supplied key and res.Signed=true, and install.sh runs as root on the node. This is the documented bootstrap-TOFU hole, and it is correctly defended by the keystone (the off-host operator credential is the real anchor and is NOT bundle-shipped, verify.go:246), so a fleet that pins an operator credential is safe even over plain HTTP. The residual exposure is the keystone-OFF + no-pubkey + no-TLS combination, which is a back-compat default rather than the recommended controller posture. The code comments (verify.go:135-140) acknowledge this; reporting it so the first-RC release notes make the TLS-or-keystone requirement non-optional for production.
confirmed_full.attack_path: Deploy in controller mode with keystone OFF and no operator --pubkey, behind a misconfigured/absent reverse proxy (plain HTTP). A man-in-the-middle on the agent<->controller path serves a forged bundle whose checksums.sha256 covers a malicious install.sh, signed by an attacker-generated key shipped as the bundle's own signing-pubkey.pem. VerifyBundle accepts it (TOFU against the bundle's key) and the node executes the rogue install.sh as root.

architecture.topic: Move LOCAL mode fully into the frontend: TypeScript reimplementation of the YAOG compile/validate/render/export pipeline, with a Go↔TS conformance harness as the central drift control
architecture.summary:
LOCAL mode today is NOT browser-resident: the React/Zustand frontend is a design tool that POSTs the topology to the Go backend's unauthenticated air-gap routes (/api/validate, /api/compile, /api/export?zip, /api/deploy-script) for every compute action (topologyStore.ts calls these; handler.go:99-310 serves them). The OWNER's goal is to make local design/validate/compile/export run entirely client-side so local mode never touches the backend. The OWNER has chosen a TypeScript reimplementation over Go→WASM, so this plan covers THAT path.

I read the entire local compile path end to end. It is a clean, downstream-only pipeline with one true browser blocker. The pipeline (internal/render/render.go All() is the orchestration entry; cmd/compiler and handler.go both route through it):
1. Schema validation — internal/validator/schema.go (411 ln): per-section structural checks + 5 regex charsets (node name, ssh fields, ssh key path, endpoint host, router-id MAC-48) + DoS bounds (2000 nodes/10000 edges) + alloc_schema_version forward-compat guard. NOTE: schema validation MUTATES the topology (normalizes empty domain.routing_mode→"babel" at schema.go:177-179, empty edge.transport→"udp" at schema.go:377-379) and these writes must round-trip — the TS port must replicate the normalize-then-validate ordering.
2. Semantic validation — internal/validator/semantic.go (1067 ln, GOD-FILE): ~20 sub-validators (ref integrity, IP collisions, name collisions across 3 normalized forms, effective port-range overflow, client-edge rules, mimic platform, allocation-pin completeness/range/cross-link-dedup, single-primary, parallel-link equal-cost warnings, route_policies-reserved reject). Shares link identity with the compiler via internal/linkid (PinKey/LinkKey/IsBackup) and naming via internal/naming — both leaf packages that exist specifically to keep one source of truth; the TS port must mirror both byte-for-byte.
3. IP allocation — internal/allocator/ip.go (191 ln): clears out-of-CIDR overlay IPs, then sequential lowest-free fill from domain CIDR honoring reserved_ranges. DETERMINISTIC ORDER MATTERS — iterates result[] in node-slice order, IPv4-only via uint32 math (binary.BigEndian), host-bit/overflow guards, /31 special-case.
4. Capability inference — internal/compiler/roles.go (171 ln): InferCapabilitiesFromRole (role→caps) + DeriveRoleSemantics (forwarding/inbound/babel-announce/AllowedIPs-mode). Small, pure, mechanical.
5. Peer derivation — internal/compiler/peers.go (1215 ln, GOD-FILE): the two-phase reserve-then-gap-fill allocator. THE HARDEST PART. Pass 1 folds enabled edges into link entities (primary class unify + per-backup distinct via linkid.LinkKey), reserves all complete pins, then gap-fills unpinned ports (lowest free ≥51820 per node), transit-IP pairs (pair N → network+2N+1, network+2N+2 per resolved CIDR, skipping network/broadcast), and IPv6 link-local pairs (fe80::%x hex). gap-fill iterates links SORTED BY linkKey (sort.Slice at peers.go:484) — this is the determinism contract (invariant I2/I9). Pass 2 builds PeerInfo (forward+reverse), endpoint resolution (explicit port override > peer's allocated listen port > public-endpoint fallback), keepalive logic, mimic MTU math (−12). Plus DeriveClientConfigs (single wg0, AllowedIPs = all domain CIDRs ∪ all transit CIDRs).
6. Renderers — internal/renderer/: wireguard.go (per-peer + client wg0 Go text/template), babel.go (per-node babeld.conf, role-announce + redistribute rules), sysctl.go, script.go (1486 ln GOD-FILE: two huge embedded bash install templates with mimic/SNAT/custody-splice/signing branches), deploy.go (bash + PowerShell SSH deploy-all). All use Go text/template with {{- -}} whitespace trimming and a shq (bashSingleQuote) func; escape.go has the bash/PowerShell quoting.
7. Export — internal/artifacts/export.go (296 ln) + handler.go createExportZip/tarGzDirectory/makeSelfExtractingInstaller: per-node bundle dirs, checksums.sha256 (bundlesig.Canonicalize: sha256 of each file, sorted by path, "%x  %s\n" lines), manifest.json, README, optional Ed25519 bundle.sig, then a ZIP of self-extracting tar.gz installers named via naming.SafeInstallerFileName.
8. WireGuard keygen — THE ONLY HARD BROWSER BLOCKER. internal/render/render.go:89,109,126 via wgtypes: 32 CSPRNG bytes, X25519 clamp (key[0]&=248; key[31]&=127; key[31]|=64), public = curve25519.ScalarBaseMult(priv), base64.StdEncoding. I read wgtypes/types.go:82-155 to get the exact bytes. This is the only piece that has no pure-stdlib browser equivalent and the only piece whose output (the PUBLIC key) propagates into the checksummed/signed bundle.

The dependency graph is strictly downward with no cycles (model←allocator/validator←compiler←renderer←render←artifacts), so a TS port can be built bottom-up in the same layering.
architecture.proposal:
Reimplement the air-gap compile path as a pure-TS library (frontend/src/compiler/) layered exactly like the Go side, gate it behind a feature flag, and protect against drift with a CI conformance harness that runs a fixture corpus through BOTH compilers and asserts canonical-equal output. Keep the Go backend as the authoritative oracle forever — the TS port is a client-side convenience, the Go path stays the spec.

ARCHITECTURE (mirror the Go layering — leaf packages first):
- frontend/src/compiler/model.ts — already largely exists as types/topology.ts; extend to carry the six pinned_* fields + alloc_schema_version (the omitempty arrays in controllerStore already enumerate them).
- frontend/src/compiler/linkid.ts — PinKey/LinkKey/IsBackup. Trivial (already partially mirrored in lib/normalizeEdges.ts PIN_FIELDS + linkKey logic — REUSE it).
- frontend/src/compiler/naming.ts — SafeInstallerFileName + WgInterfaceName + WgInterfaceNameForEdge. Needs SHA-256 (WebCrypto crypto.subtle.digest, async — see TRICKY) for the >15-char hashed interface path. lib/webauthn.ts already hand-rolls base64url; reuse its primitives.
- frontend/src/compiler/cidr.ts — IPv4 uint32 math (parse "a.b.c.d/n" → {network:uint32, maskBits}, contains, lowest-free), IPv6 link-local hex formatting, transit pair math. JS bitwise ops are 32-bit signed — MUST use >>> 0 to stay unsigned (the #1 IP-math footgun).
- frontend/src/compiler/validator.ts — schema + semantic. Port validator/code.go's ~90 CodeXxx as a const enum so error codes match the Go contract (the codes are surfaced over the API and localized by i18n; FE already has tValidationError). Replicate the schema NORMALIZE-then-validate mutation (routing_mode/transport defaults).
- frontend/src/compiler/allocator.ts, capabilities.ts, peers.ts — the core. peers.ts is the risk concentrate; port it last and lean hardest on conformance fixtures.
- frontend/src/compiler/renderers/*.ts — wireguard/babel/sysctl/script/deploy. Replace Go text/template with a tiny deterministic template helper OR plain template literals; the {{- -}} whitespace-trim semantics must be reproduced exactly (see TRICKY — byte-exact output).
- frontend/src/compiler/keygen.ts — @noble/curves x25519 (RECOMMENDED over WebCrypto: WebCrypto's X25519 is async, newer, and inconsistent across browsers for raw scalar-base-mult + raw key export; @noble/curves is sync, audited, tiny, and gives you x25519.getPublicKey(scalar) + scalarMultBase directly). Generate 32 bytes via crypto.getRandomValues, clamp identically, public = x25519.scalarMultBase(clampedPriv) (or getPublicKey), base64 via stdlib btoa over the raw bytes. This is the ONE place @noble is load-bearing.
- frontend/src/compiler/export.ts — bundlesig.Canonicalize (sha256 per file via WebCrypto, sort by path, "%x  %s\n"), then JSZip to assemble per-node bundles + the self-extracting-installer ZIP in-browser. Ed25519 bundle signing stays OUT of local mode (signing is opt-in via YAOG_BUNDLE_SIGNING_KEY, an air-gap env feature — local browser mode has no signing key, so signer==nil byte-identity path applies; do not port signing).
- frontend/src/compiler/index.ts — compile(topo) → CompileResult; the topologyStore swaps its fetch('/api/compile') calls for this when the flag is on.

THE CONFORMANCE HARNESS (the drift control — designed in detail in phases/risks below). A new Go test package internal/conformance/ owns a fixture corpus of topology JSON files (testdata/*.json — NONE exist today; the corpus must be authored). A CI job: (1) Go side runs each fixture through render.GenerateKeys(FIXED seed) → compiler.Compile → render.All → artifacts.Export into a temp dir, then emits a CANONICAL JSON manifest of {compiledTopology, perFileOutputs sorted by key, checksums.sha256}; (2) a `npm run conformance` script runs the SAME fixtures through the TS compiler emitting the same canonical manifest shape; (3) a comparator asserts byte-equality of every rendered file and every allocated value. Any divergence fails CI. The keygen non-determinism is neutralized by INJECTING a deterministic key source on both sides (fixed private keys baked into each fixture so GenerateKeys takes the "private key present, reuse + derive public" branch — exactly what equivalence_test.go already does at render.go case (a)); the conformance run never generates fresh keys, so the only thing that must match for keys is the PUBLIC-key DERIVATION (curve25519 of a fixed private key), which is the single cryptographic equivalence the harness pins.

components.name: WireGuard keypair generation (X25519)
components.current: internal/render/render.go:89,109,126 via golang.zx2c4.com/wireguard/wgctrl/wgtypes. GeneratePrivateKey = 32 CSPRNG bytes + clamp (key[0]&=248; key[31]&=127; key[31]|=64); PublicKey = curve25519.ScalarBaseMult; String = base64.StdEncoding. The ONLY hard WASM/browser blocker per the brief, confirmed by the wgtypes/render code read.
components.proposed: frontend/src/compiler/keygen.ts using @noble/curves/ed25519's x25519 export (x25519.scalarMultBase / getPublicKey). crypto.getRandomValues for entropy, identical clamp, btoa(String.fromCharCode(...raw)) for base64-std. RECOMMEND @noble/curves over WebCrypto: WebCrypto X25519 is async + raw-key-export support is browser-inconsistent; @noble is sync, audited (~tiny), deterministic.
components.effort: Low-Medium (the code is ~30 lines), but SECURITY-CRITICAL. The public-key derivation must be byte-exact: it is the only key value that flows into peer configs and the checksummed bundle. Pin it with a known-answer test (fixed priv → expected pub) AND the conformance harness.
components.risk: Crypto correctness: clamp-order or scalar-base-mult mismatch silently produces a wrong-but-valid-looking pubkey → bundles that fail handshake on real hardware but pass CI unless the KAT exists. Adds a runtime dependency (@noble) to a project that is proud of minimal deps. Browser CSPRNG quality is fine (getRandomValues).

components.name: IP/CIDR math (allocator + transit + link-local)
components.current: internal/allocator/ip.go (uint32 sequential fill, reserved_ranges, /31 special-case, out-of-CIDR clear) + peers.go allocateTransitPair (pair N → net+2N+1/+2; skips network+broadcast) + allocateLinkLocalPair (fe80::%x). All via Go net.IP + binary.BigEndian.Uint32 — clean unsigned 32-bit math.
components.proposed: frontend/src/compiler/cidr.ts. Parse dotted-quad to a number, mask via bit ops, but EVERY arithmetic result must be coerced with >>> 0 to unsigned 32-bit (JS bitwise is signed-32). IPv6 link-local uses lowercase hex (Go uses %x, NOT %d — a documented past bug at peers.go:1002).
components.effort: Medium. The math is small but the unsigned-coercion and the exact iteration order (lowest-free, network-slice order) are easy to get subtly wrong.
components.risk: JS signed-32 bitwise overflow (>>> 0 omitted) → wrong IPs for CIDRs above /1 boundaries or large pools. Off-by-one on the network/broadcast exclusion. Hex vs decimal in link-local. All caught by conformance fixtures that exercise non-/24 transit CIDRs and reserved ranges.

components.name: Deterministic peer derivation (two-phase reserve-then-gap-fill)
components.current: internal/compiler/peers.go (1215 ln). Fold edges→links via linkid.LinkKey (primary unify + per-backup), reserve all complete pins into per-node port / per-CIDR transit / global link-local sets, then gap-fill UNPINNED links iterated SORTED BY linkKey (sort.Slice peers.go:484), lowest-free per pool. Pass 2 builds forward+reverse PeerInfo with endpoint/keepalive/mimic-MTU resolution. Allocation identity = linkKey; direction-aware write-back of six pinned_* fields in compiler.go:161-208.
components.proposed: frontend/src/compiler/peers.ts mirroring the algorithm exactly, including the sort.Slice(links, linkKey<linkKey) ordering and the lowest-free pool scan. REUSE lib/normalizeEdges.ts which already mirrors the heal/linkKey logic.
components.effort: HIGH. This is the single largest and subtlest port. The stability invariants (I1-I10, delete/re-add idempotence, superset byte-stability) are the project's documented bug magnet and the reason allocation_stability_test.go (574 ln) + peers_endpoint_test.go (453 ln) exist.
components.risk: HIGHEST. Any difference in iteration/sort order, pin-reservation semantics, primary-class unify, or backup discrimination produces a DIFFERENT-but-internally-consistent allocation → the TS local compile and a later controller/air-gap recompile of the same topology disagree on ports/IPs, breaking incremental-growth stability silently. Mitigation: port last, fixture corpus must include parallel links, backups, pinned + unpinned mixes, multi-domain transit CIDRs, delete/re-add scenarios.

components.name: Schema + semantic validation (+ error-code contract)
components.current: validator/schema.go (411) + semantic.go (1067) + code.go (~90 CodeXxx). Schema MUTATES topo (normalizes routing_mode/transport) then validates; semantic runs ~20 cross-ref/collision/pin/parallel-link sub-validators; shares linkid + naming. DoS bounds + alloc_schema_version forward-compat fail-closed.
components.proposed: frontend/src/compiler/validator.ts + a generated code-constant enum kept in sync with code.go (the codes cross the API and are localized by FE i18n tValidationError, so they are already a shared contract). Replicate normalize-then-validate ordering and the exact 5 regex charsets.
components.effort: Medium-High (volume: 1478 combined lines of branchy logic) but mechanical — no crypto, no float, mostly string/ref/set checks.
components.risk: Drift on error CODES (FE already localizes them, so a wrong/missing code shows an untranslated/empty error). Regex charset divergence (JS vs Go regexp are mostly compatible for these simple classes, but anchoring \A/\z vs ^/$ and Unicode handling differ). Missing a sub-validator → local mode accepts a topology the backend would reject, then export produces a config that fails on the host.

components.name: Renderers (wireguard / babel / sysctl / script / deploy) — byte-exact templating
components.current: internal/renderer/*.go using Go text/template with {{- -}} whitespace trimming, a shq template func (bashSingleQuote), and large embedded bash/PowerShell heredocs (script.go 1486 ln, deploy.go 686 ln). Output is consumed by wg-quick/babeld/sha256sum and must be byte-exact (it is checksummed and, in air-gap, signed).
components.proposed: frontend/src/compiler/renderers/*.ts. Either a minimal faithful reimpl of the text/template subset used (range, if, with, {{- -}} trim, the shq func) OR convert the templates to TS template literals with explicit whitespace control. Reuse escape.ts (port bashSingleQuote/powerShellArgQuote verbatim — pure string ops).
components.effort: High (script.go alone is ~1500 lines of template). Mostly transcription, but the {{- and -}} trim rules and trailing-newline handling are where byte-exactness lives.
components.risk: Whitespace/newline divergence is the dominant byte-exact hazard — text/template's {{- chomps preceding whitespace incl. one newline; a hand-port that mishandles one trim makes checksums.sha256 differ on every node and the conformance harness goes red everywhere. Map-iteration order in RenderAllWireGuardConfigs (Go map[nodeID:iface]) is non-deterministic — the harness must compare per-file by KEY, not as a concatenated blob.

components.name: ZIP export (artifacts/export.go → JSZip) + canonical checksums
components.current: artifacts/export.go writes per-node dirs + bundlesig.Canonicalize checksums (sha256 per file, sorted by path, '%x  %s\n') + manifest.json + README; handler.go wraps each node dir as a self-extracting tar.gz inside a ZIP, named via naming.SafeInstallerFileName. Optional Ed25519 signing is opt-in and OFF in local mode.
components.proposed: frontend/src/compiler/export.ts using JSZip for the archive and WebCrypto crypto.subtle.digest('SHA-256') for the canonical checksums (sort paths by raw byte order, lowercase hex, two-space separator, single LF). SKIP the self-extracting tar.gz wrapper if it adds disproportionate cost — a plain per-node-dir ZIP is acceptable for local mode IF the harness scopes the comparison to bundle contents+checksums, not the installer wrapper bytes (decide via open question).
components.effort: Medium. JSZip is mature; the determinism risk is in Canonicalize (path sort must be raw-byte, matching Go sort.Strings) and ZIP metadata (timestamps/order — JSZip lets you fix these).
components.risk: manifest.json carries compiled_at timestamp and is deliberately OUTSIDE the checksum set — the harness must exclude it from byte-comparison or pin the clock. ZIP entry timestamps/permissions are non-content metadata; compare extracted contents, not raw ZIP bytes. The tar.gz self-extracting wrapper, if reproduced, adds gzip-determinism headaches — recommend scoping it out of local mode.

components.name: Capability inference + role semantics
components.current: internal/compiler/roles.go (171 ln): InferCapabilitiesFromRole + DeriveRoleSemantics + DeriveAllowedIPsForPeer. Pure, small, mechanical switch on role.
components.proposed: frontend/src/compiler/capabilities.ts. Direct transcription. FE already has lib/roleCapabilities — check for reuse/consolidation.
components.risk: Low. Pure function, fully covered by roles_test.go-equivalent fixtures.

components.name: Conformance harness (Go oracle + TS runner + comparator) and fixture corpus
components.current: DOES NOT EXIST. The closest today is render/equivalence_test.go (entrypoint parity, Go-only) and the heavy per-package Go tests. There is NO testdata/ topology corpus anywhere (confirmed: find . -name testdata returns nothing) and NO frontend test runner at all (no vitest, no *.test.ts).
components.proposed: internal/conformance/ Go package: emits a canonical manifest (compiled topology + per-file outputs keyed+sorted + checksums) per fixture using FIXED per-node private keys (so keygen is deterministic and only public-key DERIVATION is exercised). frontend conformance script (add vitest or a plain node runner) emits the same manifest shape. A comparator (Go test reading both, or a node script) asserts byte-equality. New CI job runs both and diffs. Authors a corpus of ~20-40 fixtures spanning every branch: each role, client edges, parallel/backup links, pinned vs unpinned allocations, multi-domain + custom transit CIDR, reserved ranges, mimic (tcp transport), MTU edge cases, name-collision near-misses, validation-failure cases.
components.effort: High and FOUNDATIONAL — this is the actual deliverable that makes the rewrite safe. Build it BEFORE/ALONGSIDE the port, not after.
components.risk: If the corpus under-covers a branch, drift in that branch ships silently. The harness is only as good as the fixtures. Keygen determinism MUST be solved by key injection (fixed keys per fixture) or the harness is non-deterministic and useless. Map-iteration and whitespace comparison must be content-canonical, not raw-byte.

phases: Phase 0 — Conformance scaffold FIRST (de-risks everything). Author the internal/conformance/ Go oracle that runs the existing pipeline (render.GenerateKeys with FIXED fixture keys → Compile → All → Export) and emits a canonical per-fixture manifest (compiled topology JSON + per-file outputs sorted by key + checksums.sha256). Seed the fixture corpus with ~10 fixtures (one per role, one client, one parallel/backup, one multi-domain custom-transit-CIDR, two validation-failure cases). Add a CI job that snapshots the Go manifests (golden) so the oracle itself is pinned. NO TS yet. Outcome: a machine-checkable definition of 'correct output' independent of the TS work.

Phase 1 — Leaf layer in TS + keygen KAT. Port model.ts, linkid.ts, naming.ts (needs WebCrypto SHA-256), cidr.ts, capabilities.ts, escape.ts, and keygen.ts. Land a known-answer test for keygen (fixed 32-byte private key → expected base64 public key, generated once from the Go side) and run @noble x25519 against it. Wire vitest (or a node runner) into frontend CI. Outcome: the deterministic primitives are proven equal to Go.

Phase 2 — Validators in TS, conformance-gated. Port validator.ts (schema normalize-then-validate + semantic) and the code-constant enum. Expand the fixture corpus with all validation-pass and validation-fail cases. The harness now asserts: TS validation verdict + error-code SET == Go for every fixture. Outcome: local mode can validate without the backend, drift-proof.

Phase 3 — Allocator + peer derivation (the hard core), conformance-gated. Port allocator.ts then peers.ts (two-phase reserve-then-gap-fill, linkKey-sorted gap-fill, direction-aware pin write-back). Expand fixtures heavily here: pinned/unpinned mixes, delete/re-add stability, superset growth, backup discrimination, transit-pool exhaustion edges. The harness asserts every allocated value (overlay IPs, ports, transit IPs, link-locals, interface names, pins) byte-equal to Go. Outcome: the determinism-critical layer is proven equal.

Phase 4 — Renderers + export in TS, byte-exact conformance-gated. Port the five renderers (faithful text/template-subset helper or template literals with exact {{- -}} trim) and export.ts (Canonicalize + JSZip). The harness now asserts every rendered file byte-equal and the full checksums.sha256 equal. Decide the self-extracting-installer-wrapper scope (open question). Outcome: full client-side compile+export, byte-equal to the backend.

Phase 5 — Wire into the store behind a flag + cutover. Add a feature flag (default OFF) that makes topologyStore.compile()/exportArtifacts()/validate()/deployScript() call the TS library instead of fetching the air-gap routes. Run both in parallel in a canary period (compute locally AND fetch the backend, diff in dev builds, report mismatches). Once the conformance harness is green across the corpus and the canary shows zero diffs on real designs, flip the flag default ON and document that the air-gap routes remain for controller mode + as the conformance oracle. Outcome: local mode never touches the backend; Go path retained as the authoritative spec + CI oracle.

Phase 6 — Lock the drift gate. Make the conformance job a REQUIRED CI check on every PR (Go or TS). Add a guard test that fails if a new validator CodeXxx is added in Go without a corresponding TS constant, and if a renderer template changes without a refreshed golden. Document in docs/spec that the Go pipeline is the spec and any change must update fixtures.
risks: BYTE-EXACTNESS via whitespace: Go text/template {{- -}} trim and trailing-newline rules are the dominant hazard. A single mishandled trim in script.go's ~1500-line template makes checksums.sha256 differ on every node. Mitigation: port the actual {{- -}} chomp semantics in a tiny shared template helper rather than ad-hoc literals; the harness catches it immediately but only if fixtures exercise the branch.

ALLOCATION DRIFT (highest-severity correctness risk): peers.ts must reproduce the linkKey-sorted gap-fill and reserve-then-fill order exactly, or the TS local compile and a later controller/air-gap recompile of the SAME topology disagree on ports/transit-IPs/pins — breaking the documented incremental-growth stability invariants (I1-I10) silently. The two paths producing different-but-valid allocations is worse than a crash. Mitigation: port last, fixtures must cover delete/re-add + superset + parallel/backup + pinned/unpinned.

CRYPTO MISMATCH: a clamp-order or scalar-base-mult error in keygen.ts yields a wrong-but-plausible public key → bundles that fail WireGuard handshake on real hardware but look fine in CI. Mitigation: keygen KAT (fixed priv→known pub from Go) + the conformance harness derives public keys from fixed fixture private keys.
JS 32-bit signed bitwise: omitting >>> 0 in cidr.ts corrupts IP math for large pools. Mitigation: a CIDR unit test sweep + fixtures with non-/24 transit CIDRs and reserved ranges.

ERROR-CODE / VALIDATION DRIFT: the FE already localizes validator codes via i18n; a missing/renamed code in the TS port shows an empty/untranslated error, and a missing sub-validator lets local mode accept a topology the backend rejects (then export fails on the host). Mitigation: generate the TS code enum from code.go, add a sync-guard test, fixtures for every validation-fail case.

DUAL-MAINTENANCE BURDEN (the strategic risk of choosing TS-reimpl over WASM): every future change to the Go pipeline (a new validator, a template tweak, a model field) now needs a parallel TS change or the conformance gate goes red. This is permanent overhead. The harness makes drift LOUD but does not make it FREE. The OWNER chose this tradeoff knowingly; the mitigation is to make the conformance gate a required check and add sync-guard tests so omissions fail CI, not production.

CORPUS UNDER-COVERAGE: the harness is only as strong as the fixtures; an un-exercised branch can drift silently. There is no fixture corpus today (none found in repo). Mitigation: derive fixtures from the existing per-package Go tests' inline topologies (compiler_test/peers_endpoint_test/parallel_links_test/mimic_test all build representative topologies) and require a fixture for every code path touched.

DEPENDENCY/BUNDLE-SIZE creep: @noble/curves + JSZip add to the frontend bundle and the dependency surface of a deliberately-minimal project (go.sum is 10 lines; FE already has a dead zod dep). Mitigation: @noble is tiny and audited; JSZip is only loaded on export (dynamic import). Confirm with the OWNER that adding these two deps is acceptable.

SECRET/CUSTODY surface shift: today local-mode private keys round-trip through the backend air-gap compile (AirGap custody, render.go case a/c). Moving keygen client-side means real private keys are generated and held in the browser/localStorage. This is arguably BETTER (never leaves the machine) but changes the threat model — localStorage now holds real WG private keys in local mode. Mitigation: document it; it matches the air-gap intent (keys are meant to be machine-local) but the OWNER should confirm.
open_questions: Self-extracting installer scope: handler.go wraps each node bundle as a self-extracting tar.gz inside the export ZIP (makeSelfExtractingInstaller). Reproducing gzip+tar+shell-wrapper byte-exactly in-browser is disproportionate effort with gzip-determinism headaches. Is a plain per-node-directory ZIP acceptable for local-mode export, with the conformance harness comparing bundle CONTENTS + checksums.sha256 rather than the wrapper bytes? (Strong recommendation: yes, scope the wrapper out.)

Conformance comparison granularity: compare the canonical content manifest (compiled topology + per-file outputs keyed + checksums) — NOT raw ZIP bytes (timestamps/order are non-content metadata) and NOT manifest.json (carries compiled_at). Confirm this canonical-content definition of 'equal' is acceptable, or does the OWNER want raw-archive-byte equality (which forces pinning the clock + ZIP metadata)?

Keygen library: @noble/curves (sync, audited, tiny, deterministic raw scalar-base-mult — RECOMMENDED) vs WebCrypto SubtleCrypto X25519 (async, newer, browser-inconsistent raw-key export, zero-dep). Recommend @noble. Confirm adding @noble/curves + JSZip as runtime deps is acceptable given the project's minimal-dependency ethos.

Local-mode mimic catalog: HandleCompile/Export read render.FetchSettingsFromEnv() (a server-side env feature for local-mode mimic .deb pins). The browser has no env. Drop client-side fetch-settings entirely (zero FetchSettings → byte-identical bundle, the air-gap default) and document that local-mode mimic pinning requires the backend? (Recommendation: drop it; it is rarely used and the zero value is already the byte-identity baseline.)

Validation-as-you-type vs on-demand: once validation is client-side and instant, should the canvas validate live (better UX) or keep the explicit Validate action? This changes how much of the TS validator runs per keystroke (the O(n^2) collision passes have a 2000-node DoS bound that is irrelevant client-side but still costs CPU). Out of scope for correctness but affects the Phase-2 wiring.

Cutover strategy: hard flip (flag default ON after green harness) vs a canary period where local mode computes BOTH locally and via the backend and reports diffs in dev builds before trusting TS. Recommend the canary — it catches corpus-under-coverage on REAL user designs, not just fixtures. Confirm the OWNER wants the canary phase (adds latency in local mode temporarily) or prefers a clean flag flip gated purely on the harness.
architecture.current_state: LOCAL mode is a thin design client that delegates ALL compute to the Go backend's unauthenticated air-gap routes; the frontend has no client-side compiler, no test runner, and no fixture corpus. The Go compile path is a clean, cycle-free, downward-layered pipeline (model→allocator/validator→compiler→renderer→render→artifacts) with exactly one hard browser blocker: WireGuard X25519 keygen (render.go via wgtypes). render.All() is the single shared entry point that both cmd/compiler and the API route through, and render/equivalence_test.go already proves entry-point parity Go-side. The determinism-critical surfaces are peers.go (two-phase linkKey-sorted reserve-then-gap-fill, 1215 ln, the documented bug magnet guarded by allocation_stability_test.go/peers_endpoint_test.go) and the renderers' byte-exact text/template output (which is checksummed by bundlesig.Canonicalize and, in air-gap, Ed25519-signed). Schema validation mutates the topology (normalizing routing_mode/transport) and that mutation must round-trip. Allocation determinism rests on slice/sort order, not map order; the WG-config map is keyed by nodeID:iface and iterates non-deterministically, so any conformance comparison must be per-file-by-key, not whole-blob. The exact keygen contract (clamp + curve25519 scalar-base-mult + base64-std) is confirmed from wgtypes/types.go:82-155.

architecture.proposal:
REMOVE four air-gap routes + gateAirgap (server.go 87-120) and handlers/installer (handler.go 100-583); keep HandleHealth, relocate envelope. cmd/server controller-only. KEEP Go compiler: controller/compile.go:138-168 uses same GenerateKeys->Compile->render.All (AgentHeld); cmd/compiler keeps AirGap. Static SPA design + controller binary fleet. Security delta: unauth compute/keygen oracle, 4MiB unauth DoS, gateAirgap, root-script installer all vanish. Two-compiler tax permanent; add cross-language conformance harness (FE has no test runner).</parameter>
</invoke>

architecture.current_layout_assessment:
The package DEPENDENCY GRAPH is healthy and the bounded-context split is largely correct. `go build ./...` is clean and Go forbids import cycles, so there are NO cyclic deps; the real import graph (via `go list`) is strictly downward: model/apierr/bundlesig/trustlist/naming are leaves; allocator/linkid/normalize/validator/compiler/renderer/artifacts/render sit in the middle; controller and api are the two sinks (controller -> compiler/render/renderer/artifacts/trustlist/bundlesig/normalize; api -> controller + everything). No backwards edge exists (e.g. controller does NOT import api; renderer does not import render). The api/controller/compiler separation is genuinely good: compiler+validator+allocator+renderer+render form a self-contained, stdlib-only, browser-portable pipeline (only render.go's wgtypes keygen is the WASM blocker), controller owns persistence+keystone+staging, api owns only HTTP/auth/wire. Test mass (~1.2:1 test:src) is heavy and well-targeted; test FILES in api are actually split by concern (handler_login_test, handler_passkey_test, handler_totp_test, keystone_rotation_test...) BETTER than the production code they cover.

The real structural problems are localized, not systemic: (1) two genuine god-files — controller/filestore.go (1974 ln) and api/handler_controller.go (1964 ln); (2) the render vs renderer naming pair is a documented readability trap whose root cause is a single MISPLACED data type (renderer.Artifact lives in the template package only to dodge a would-be cycle); (3) compile.go (955 ln) blends three concerns (frozen-pipeline driver, keystone manifest/epoch engine, keystone verify/rotation) with an obvious keystone.go seam; (4) the ~30 wire-type structs and both route tables inline in handler_controller.go inflate the god-file and scatter the wire contract. None of these are correctness or dependency-direction defects — they are clarity/maintainability hotspots and diff-review friction. The fixes are mostly mechanical, intra-package, non-breaking file moves; only the render/renderer rename touches import paths.

problems.issue: Single 1964-line file mixes FIVE distinct concerns: the entire agent surface (HandleEnroll/Config/Poll/Report/Rekey, lines 587-816,1488), the operator deploy surface (UpdateTopology/Stage/CompilePreview/Promote/Nodes/Revoke/ClearRekey/Audit/Topology(/Versions)/EnrollmentToken/RekeyAll, 816-1488), the keystone signing trio (OperatorCredential/TrustList/TrustListSignature, 1584-1903), both route tables (RegisterAgentRoutes 195, RegisterOperatorRoutes 213), the credentialed CORS (320), ~30 inline wire-type structs (347-585, plus 922), and shared helpers (identity/decodeJSON/readControllerBody/parseAfter, 1904-1962). The login/totp/passkey/bootstrap/cookie/release-pins handlers were already split into sibling files but the core dispatch was not — this is the region's primary structural risk and the 2nd-largest file in the codebase.

1974-line file is the entire JSON-on-disk Store impl (69 methods) in one unit — nodes, topology records, signed bundles, audit, operator creds, served/staged trustlist split, sessions, settings, signing anchor. memstore.go (896) implements the SAME ~38-method Store interface a second time, kept in lockstep only by a shared store_compat_test.go (1441 ln). Largest file in the codebase; high diff-review and drift surface.

Two packages one letter apart, both imported by handler.go (render = orchestration+keygen+signing entry; renderer = leaf text/template emitters). The names give no hint which is the entry point. ROOT CAUSE of the confusion is concrete and fixable: the shared DATA type renderer.Artifact (a plain {Asset,SHA256} value struct — NOT a renderer) was deliberately placed in the template package ONLY to dodge a render->renderer->render cycle (its own doc comment says so). As a result controller/store.go, handler_bootstrap.go, and release_pins.go all import the low-level renderer package solely for renderer.Artifact — they have no business depending on the install-script template package. This is the same 'wrong home to break a cycle' smell that linkid was hoisted out to solve elsewhere.
problems.evidence: internal/renderer/fetch.go:11-24 (Artifact doc: 'Defined in package renderer ... so render can reference it without the render -> renderer -> render import cycle'); render.go:143-146 (type Artifact = renderer.Artifact alias); store.go:332,346; handler_bootstrap.go:46,51; release_pins.go:175,308,322 all import renderer ONLY for Artifact

architecture.proposed_structure:
Keep the existing PACKAGE topology — it is correct. Do NOT split internal/api or internal/controller into sub-packages (that would force exporting today-private helpers like identity/decodeJSON/requireNode and the unexported Store fields, trading a file-size smell for an export-surface smell, and Go has no sub-package privacy). The leverage is in (a) FILE decomposition within the two god-packages and (b) ONE small package rename that resolves the render/renderer trap at its root.

Target file layout (intra-package, no new sub-packages):

internal/api/  (split handler_controller.go ~1964 -> ~6 cohesive files; package stays `api`)
  routes_controller.go   - ControllerHandler struct, NewControllerHandler, RegisterAgentRoutes, RegisterOperatorRoutes, path-prefix setters, cors, originAllowed (the wiring + struct)
  handler_agent.go       - HandleEnroll/Config/Poll/Report/Rekey (the agent surface)
  handler_deploy.go      - HandleUpdateTopology/Stage/CompilePreview/Promote/Nodes/Revoke/ClearRekey/Audit/Topology(/Versions)/EnrollmentToken/RekeyAll (operator deploy/fleet CRUD)
  handler_keystone.go    - HandleOperatorCredential(+Status/+Pin)/HandleTrustList/HandleTrustListSignature/stagedManifest/pinFromParts/pinFromCredential/auditKeystone (keystone signing flow)
  wire_controller.go     - the ~30 *JSON request/response structs (the surveyable wire contract)
  helpers_controller.go  - identity/decodeJSON/readControllerBody/parseAfter/topologyHasNode/isReservedNodeID
  (handler.go air-gap, auth_controller.go, cookie_session.go, handler_login/passkey/totp/bootstrap.go, release_pins.go, loginratelimit.go, server.go, static.go stay as-is)

internal/controller/  (split compile.go ~955 -> 2 files; optionally thin filestore.go; package stays `controller`)
  compile.go    - CompileSubgraph/CompileAndStage/PromoteStaged/persistAllocations/readBundleDir/enrolledSubgraph/BuildFetchSettings/AgentRolloutNodeIDs (the staging pipeline)
  keystone.go   - bundleSHA256/manifestMembers/sameMembership/stageManifest/enforceSigningAnchor + pinFromOperatorCredential/KeystoneFingerprint/SameKeystoneCredential/verifyStoredAgainstPin/KeystoneRedeployRequired (the membership/epoch + credential engine)
  filestore.go  - OPTIONAL later: split by data domain (filestore_audit.go, filestore_trustlist.go, filestore_session.go) only if it keeps growing; lower priority than compile.go because it is one cohesive impl.

Package rename to kill the render/renderer trap (the one BREAKING change worth doing):
  internal/renderer -> internal/template   (the leaf text/template emitters: wireguard/babel/script/deploy/sysctl/escape)
  internal/render    stays render          (the orchestration + keygen + signing entry point)
  AND move the shared data types Artifact + InstallFetch + the transit-CIDR/SNAT constant OUT of the template package. Two clean options: (i) put Artifact/InstallFetch in internal/model (they are pure value types in the wire/data domain, already JSON-tagged, consumed by controller/api/render) — this removes the only reason controller/store.go, handler_bootstrap.go, release_pins.go import the template package at all; (ii) keep the transit-CIDR constant in model so compiler/validator/template/deploy reference ONE source instead of three copies + four literals.

After this, the name pair becomes render (orchestrator) -> template (emitters), which reads correctly, and three importers drop their spurious dependency on the leaf template package.

refactors.rationale: Cuts the region's primary god-file from 1964 lines to ~6 files of <500 lines each, each a single concern (agent surface / operator deploy / keystone / wire contract / wiring / helpers). All symbols stay in package api, so NO export-surface change and NO import-path change — purely moving func bodies and struct decls between files in the same dir. Mirrors the already-good per-concern split of the TEST files (handler_login_test, handler_passkey_test, keystone_rotation_test...) that the production code failed to follow.

refactors.rationale: Makes the HTTP wire contract surveyable in one file, which is the single artifact a frontend author and the hand-synced TS mirrors (types/controller.ts) most need. Done as part of or alongside refactor 1. Same package, zero behavior change.

Reduces a 955-line approaching-god-file and makes the load-bearing anti-rollback/served-vs-staged logic (the recently bug-fixed beta.5 surface) easier to review in isolation. refactors.rationale: After the file/package moves, the only doc that maps the source tree is already badly stale (8 of 17 packages, missing internal/render entirely) and will be more so.
