sequencing: PHASE 1 (mechanical, low-risk, non-breaking, do first and independently — these are pure intra-package file moves that a single `go build ./... && go test ./...` validates): refactor 1 (split handler_controller.go), refactor 2 (wire_controller.go), refactor 3 (split compile.go -> keystone.go). None change import paths or exported symbols, so they can land as separate commits with zero blast radius and make the subsequent diffs far easier to review. Recommend doing these before any rename so the rename diff stays small.

PHASE 2 (low-risk, non-breaking, but cross-package — do before the rename): refactor 4 (move Artifact/InstallFetch + transit-CIDR const into internal/model and drop the spurious renderer imports in controller/store.go, handler_bootstrap.go, release_pins.go). This must precede the rename because it shrinks what the to-be-renamed package exports and reduces the number of import sites the rename touches. Validate with `go build ./...` + `go test ./...` + a `git diff` check that the air-gap byte-identity tests (render/equivalence_test.go, export_test.go, script_signature_test.go) still pass — these assert the install bundle is byte-unchanged, so they are the safety net for any move that touches the render/template path.

PHASE 3 (the one BREAKING change — do alone, last, behind a green Phase 1+2): refactor 5 (rename internal/renderer -> internal/template). Mechanical but rewrites import paths across ~19 files; run as a single atomic commit with `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` as the gate, and update the frontend-irrelevant doc citations in the same commit. Because it is breaking only at the INTERNAL import level (no external consumers — internal/ is unimportable outside the module), the blast radius is fully contained and CI catches any missed site.

PHASE 4 (cleanup + docs, any time after, low-risk): refactor 6 (remove dead exported surface — watch for test references to the unsigned renderers), refactor 8 (refresh repository-structure.md + spec README mTLS line). refactor 7 (filestore.go split) is explicitly DEFERRED / not recommended now to avoid churn the owner asked to avoid.

RISK NOTE: every Phase 1-3 step is independently revertable and gated by the existing ~1.2:1 test suite (`go test ./...`) plus the air-gap byte-identity assertions; the keystone regression suite (internal/regression/keystone_regression_test.go) and store_compat_test.go are the load-bearing nets for the compile.go/keystone.go split and any controller-side move — run them explicitly. Do NOT attempt sub-packaging internal/api or internal/controller: it would force exporting today-private helpers (identity, decodeJSON, requireNode, unexported Store fields) and trade a file-length smell for a worse export-surface smell, with no dependency-direction benefit since the graph is already acyclic.

arch_skeptic.overall:
The proposal is unusually honest and technically accurate — I verified its load-bearing claims against the code and they hold: god-file line counts (semantic.go 1067, peers.go 1215, script.go 1486), no testdata corpus, no frontend test runner, the keygen single-blocker at render.go:89/109/126, the schema NORMALIZE-then-validate mutation (schema.go:177-179, 377-379), and the linkKey-sorted gap-fill determinism contract (peers.go:484). It correctly identifies peers.ts as the risk concentrate and correctly grounds the harness's keygen-determinism trick in what equivalence_test.go already does (inject fixed privkeys -> case (a) reuse). The drift control is sound IN PRINCIPLE and the phasing (harness FIRST, port last) is the right order.

My core skepticism is NOT about whether the plan is buildable — it is. It is about whether the OWNER'S CHOSEN PATH (full TS reimplementation) is worth a PERMANENT dual-compiler maintenance tax versus the alternative the owner rejected (Go->WASM), and whether the conformance harness — which is real, foundational work that does not exist yet (zero fixtures, zero TS test runner) — can actually be kept honest over time rather than just at the moment of cutover. The honest framing the proposal buries: this converts a ~5800-line single-source-of-truth Go pipeline into TWO implementations of the same spec that must stay byte-identical forever, gated by a fixture corpus that is only as good as its branch coverage. That is a strategic cost, not a phase.

On drift: I am setting drift_risk_adequately_mitigated = FALSE. Not because the harness design is wrong, but because the plan's own framing treats 'green at cutover' as the win condition. The proposal already has a live, UNGUARDED Go/TS drift surface today — frontend/src/lib/normalizeEdges.ts already hand-mirrors linkKey + PIN_FIELDS from peers.go/linkid with NO test pinning them equal. That is the canary: duplication-drift is already happening organically in this exact subsystem and nobody is catching it. The harness must retroactively pin that existing duplication too, and the plan does not call that out. A harness that proves equality on a fixed corpus is necessary but not sufficient — drift ships in the branch the corpus does not cover, and Phase 6's 'guard test that fails if a new CodeXxx lacks a TS constant' is the only structural (non-fixture) guard proposed, against 190 Code* references and a 1067-line semantic validator with ~20 sub-validators. Mitigatable, but the plan must commit to specific structural guards + a coverage floor BEFORE I'd call drift adequately mitigated.

I am NOT flagging the structure-refactor concern the brief hinted at (internal/render vs internal/renderer naming smell) as worth touching mid-beta — see over_engineering_flags. Net: the TS port is defensible IF the owner accepts the permanent dual-maintenance tax with eyes open and the harness is treated as a permanent required gate with structural guards, not a one-time cutover check. I'd push back hard on doing this at all during an active beta (PRs #109-#135 in the last two days) unless local-mode-offline is a hard near-term requirement.

concerns.proposal: TypeScript reimplementation of the full compile pipeline (vs the rejected Go->WASM path)
concerns.concern: This is the central strategic decision and the proposal under-argues WHY TS beats WASM, then proceeds as if the choice is settled. The owner picked TS, but the proposal's own component table is the strongest argument AGAINST it: keygen needs @noble/curves (a new audited-but-external crypto dep doing security-critical X25519, with a clamp/scalar-base-mult byte-exactness hazard that a 'wrong-but-valid-looking pubkey passes CI unless a KAT exists'), the renderers need a hand-rolled reimpl of Go text/template's {{- -}} chomp semantics across 92 trim markers and ~1500 lines of embedded bash in script.go, and peers.go's 1215-line two-phase allocator must be reproduced byte-identically. Go->WASM eliminates ALL of that: keygen, templates, allocator, validators compile from the SAME source that stays authoritative — zero dual-maintenance, zero drift surface, zero conformance harness needed for everything except the WASM boundary marshalling. The proposal dismisses WASM in one clause ('owner has chosen TS') but the entire risk section it then writes is a catalog of costs that WASM does not incur. The real tradeoffs WASM has (bundle size ~1-3MB, the wgtypes/wgctrl dependency needing a build-tag shim or replacement for js/wasm since it imports netlink, slower cold start) are NOT mentioned — so the owner may have chosen TS without seeing that the thing being traded away is the ENTIRE drift-control apparatus this proposal then spends its bulk building.
concerns.recommendation: Before committing, force an explicit written WASM-vs-TS tradeoff with the owner that puts the dual-maintenance tax on the WASM side of the ledger: WASM = one source of truth + a thin marshalling harness; TS = two sources of truth + a permanent byte-exact conformance corpus + @noble + a template-engine reimpl. If the owner's reason for TS is real (e.g. wants the compiler readable/debuggable in-browser, wants zero-Go-in-the-bundle for a future pure-frontend product, or wgctrl/netlink genuinely cannot be shimmed for js/wasm cleanly), document it and proceed. But do NOT let 'owner chose TS' bury the fact that the conformance harness exists ONLY because TS was chosen — it is not free engineering, it is the price of the rejected alternative being avoided. Quantify the wgtypes/wgctrl js/wasm blocker specifically (it imports golang.org/x/sys netlink paths) since that is the one technical fact that could legitimately tip the decision to TS.

concerns.proposal: CI conformance harness as the central drift control (internal/conformance/ + TS runner + comparator)
concerns.concern: The harness is necessary, correctly ordered (Phase 0 first), and its keygen-determinism trick is grounded (equivalence_test.go already injects fixed privkeys to hit render.go case (a)). But the plan treats 'green across the corpus at cutover' as sufficient, and it is not. Three gaps: (1) Coverage is unbounded-by-construction — '~20-40 fixtures spanning every branch' is an aspiration; against a 1067-line semantic.go with ~20 sub-validators, a 1215-line allocator with documented I1-I10 stability invariants (docs/spec/compiler/allocation-stability.md), and 190 Code* references, branch coverage will silently lag and drift ships in the uncovered branch. (2) The plan proposes only ONE structural (non-fixture) guard: Phase 6's 'fail if a new Go CodeXxx lacks a TS constant.' That leaves every renderer template change, every new allocator pool, every new sub-validator relying on a human remembering to add a fixture. (3) There is ALREADY unguarded Go/TS drift in this exact subsystem: frontend/src/lib/normalizeEdges.ts hand-mirrors linkKey + PIN_FIELDS from peers.go/linkid.go with no test pinning them equal — the plan does not even acknowledge this existing duplication, let alone fold it into the harness. If duplication-drift is already happening uncaught in the one place TS already mirrors Go, the harness must be designed to catch THAT class, not just future ports.
concerns.recommendation: (a) Make the harness a REQUIRED CI check from Phase 0 and define a coverage FLOOR, not a fixture count: instrument Go-side branch/line coverage of the pipeline packages DURING the conformance run and fail if any pipeline package drops below a threshold the corpus must maintain — so adding a Go branch without a fixture turns the harness red mechanically. (b) Add structural guards beyond the CodeXxx check: a generated manifest of (renderer template hashes, validator code set, allocator pool definitions) checked into the repo, with a test that fails when the Go side changes it without a corresponding fixture refresh — this is the 'golden that must be refreshed' the plan mentions for renderers, generalized to every layer. (c) RETROFIT the existing normalizeEdges.ts duplication into the harness as the first fixture-backed equality assertion, and treat its current unguarded state as the proof-of-concept that drift is real here. (d) Keep the dev-build canary (compute-local-AND-fetch-backend, diff) as a PERMANENT diagnostic, not just a cutover-window tool — it is the only thing that catches drift on REAL user designs the corpus never imagined.

concerns.proposal: keygen.ts via @noble/curves x25519, byte-exact public-key derivation
concerns.concern: Correctly flagged by the proposal as security-critical with a KAT, but two things are understated. First, the 'minimal deps' framing is wrong-targeted: the brief's minimal-deps pride is about the GO backend (only wgctrl); the frontend ALREADY ships React 19, React Flow, Tailwind v4, Zod, dagre, diff — @noble adds negligible bundle/audit cost there, so the proposal's own 'adds a dep to a minimal-deps project' risk is a non-issue for the frontend and should be dropped (it actually weakens the case by raising a false alarm). Second, the genuinely dangerous property is correct and worth escalating: a clamp-order or scalar-base-mult mismatch produces a pubkey that is wrong but base64-valid and config-valid — it passes every test EXCEPT a real WireGuard handshake on hardware, and that pubkey flows into the checksummed (and air-gap-signed) bundle. The KAT pins derivation against ONE fixed privkey; that is necessary but a single KAT does not exercise clamp edge cases (high-bit, low-3-bits).
concerns.recommendation: Use multiple KAT vectors (RFC 7748 test vectors plus several fixtures whose privkeys exercise the clamp boundary bits) rather than one fixed key. Cross-check the TS pubkey against the Go wgtypes output for the SAME bytes in the harness (the conformance run already does this — make keygen its first assertion). Drop the '@noble violates minimal deps' risk line; it is a Go-ethos concern, not a frontend one, and citing it invites the wrong objection. Confirm @noble's x25519.scalarMultBase applies clamping internally or NOT — if it clamps internally and the Go path also clamps, double-clamp is idempotent for X25519 but VERIFY rather than assume.

concerns.proposal: Scope the self-extracting tar.gz installer wrapper OUT of local mode; emit a plain per-node-dir ZIP
concerns.concern: This is the right call to AVOID gzip/tar determinism headaches (the wrapper at handler.go:470 embeds a SHA-256 pin, an optional Ed25519 signing branch — off in local mode — and openssl-verify bash). But it creates an output ASYMMETRY: the air-gap backend export and the local-mode export now produce DIFFERENT artifacts (self-extracting installer vs plain ZIP). The proposal hand-waves this as 'decide via open question' and 'scope the harness to bundle contents+checksums, not installer wrapper bytes.' That means the harness STOPS being byte-exact at exactly the layer (export packaging) where a user actually receives the deliverable, and the two modes diverge in what the operator runs on the host. A user who exports locally then later re-exports via a controller gets a structurally different artifact — surprising, and the install UX (double-click self-extractor vs unzip-then-run) differs silently.
concerns.recommendation: Acceptable to scope out, but make the divergence EXPLICIT and bounded: (a) the harness must still assert byte-equality of every file INSIDE the bundle and the checksums.sha256 (it can, since the wrapper is just packaging around identical contents); (b) document in docs/spec that local mode emits unwrapped bundles by design and the self-extracting installer is a controller/air-gap-only convenience; (c) reconsider whether JSZip can reproduce the tar.gz deterministically — gzip with a fixed mtime/OS byte and no filename header IS reproducible, and tar with fixed mtime/uid/gid is reproducible, so 'too hard' may be overstated. If reproducible-tar.gz is ~a day of work, doing it keeps the modes symmetric and avoids a permanent caveat. Decide deliberately, do not leave it as an open question that resolves itself by omission.

concerns.proposal: Renderers in TS: faithful text/template subset OR template literals with manual {{- -}} trim
concerns.concern: This is the largest transcription surface (script.go alone 1486 lines, 92 trim markers, 122 template-func refs across renderers) and the dominant byte-exact hazard the proposal correctly names. My added concern: the proposal offers TWO mutually-exclusive strategies (reimplement a text/template subset vs convert to template literals) without committing, and they have very different drift profiles. A faithful text/template-subset ENGINE means future Go template edits port over as near-identical template text (low ongoing drift). Hand-converting to TS template literals means EVERY future Go template change must be MANUALLY re-translated into a structurally different TS literal (high ongoing drift, and the {{- chomp must be re-reasoned each time). The plan picks neither, so the highest-churn renderer (script.go's install bash) could end up as the most drift-prone hand-translation.
concerns.recommendation: COMMIT to the faithful text/template-subset engine for the renderers, not template literals. Reason: it minimizes ongoing translation drift — a Go template diff maps to a near-identical TS template diff, and the chomp semantics live in ONE tested helper rather than being re-reasoned per literal. Pay the one-time cost of a small {{- -}}/range/if/with/shq engine (this is a few hundred lines, well-trodden) so that script.go's template TEXT can be shared/near-copied rather than rewritten. Pin the engine itself with its own unit tests (chomp before/after, trailing newline) independent of the fixtures. If the team genuinely cannot afford the engine, then renderers are the strongest argument to revisit WASM (which renders from the identical Go template, zero drift).

concerns.proposal: Overall sequencing: undertake this during an active beta
concerns.concern: Memory and git history show intense in-flight beta work: PRs #109-#135 merged in the last ~2-3 days, current branch test/keystone-regression-suite, v2.0.0-beta.7 is GitHub-latest as of yesterday, and there are owed manual smokes (real-host rotation/restart, passkey, browser). Dropping a 6-phase, multi-package rewrite that adds a frontend test runner (none exists), a new Go conformance package, and a new crypto dep ON TOP of an unsettled beta risks destabilizing the thing being stabilized — and the conformance oracle depends on the Go pipeline being FROZEN enough to be 'the spec,' which it is not while beta bugs are still being fixed in peers.go/semantic.go/script.go.
concerns.recommendation: Sequence this AFTER beta stabilizes (or at minimum gate it entirely behind the default-OFF flag with zero changes to the existing air-gap routes, which the plan does do — good). Build Phase 0 (the Go-side oracle + fixture corpus) NOW even during beta, because it has independent value: it is a golden-output regression net for the Go pipeline itself and would have caught past pin-collision / allocation-drift bugs (the very class beta.5-beta.7 fixed). But do NOT start the TS port (Phases 1-5) until the pipeline source is quiet enough to be a stable oracle — porting against a moving target multiplies rework. Frame Phase 0 as 'pipeline golden-regression suite' (ships value regardless of whether the TS port ever happens) and make Phases 1-5 a separate go/no-go gated on beta exit + the WASM-vs-TS writeup.

concerns.proposal: Error-code contract synchronization (validator/code.go -> generated TS enum)
concerns.concern: 190 Code* references; FE already localizes them via tValidationError, so a missing/wrong TS code yields an empty or untranslated error in local mode — a silent UX failure, not a loud one. The plan's Phase-6 guard (fail if a Go CodeXxx lacks a TS constant) is the right idea but is listed LAST, after the validators are already ported in Phase 2. That ordering lets the validator port proceed for several phases with manually-synced codes before the mechanical guard arrives.
concerns.recommendation: Generate the TS code enum from code.go (or a shared source) MECHANICALLY in Phase 1/2, not hand-maintain it, and land the 'Go code without TS constant fails CI' guard in the SAME phase the codes first cross over — not deferred to Phase 6. A codegen step (Go code.go -> codes.ts) is cheap and eliminates the entire hand-sync drift class for the one contract that is already shared over the API and localized. This is the single highest-leverage structural guard in the whole plan; move it early.

over_engineering_flags: Structure re-design (internal/render vs internal/renderer naming smell): the brief asks to assess it, but renaming/merging these two packages mid-beta is churn-for-its-own-sake with real risk — internal/render is the orchestration entry (All/GenerateKeys) and internal/renderer holds the leaf renderers; the layering is actually correct, only the names are confusable. Renaming touches import paths across cmd/compiler, handler.go, render.go, and every renderer test. Do NOT refactor this now; at most add a one-line package doc comment to each clarifying the render(orchestrator)-vs-renderer(leaves) split. Defer any rename to a dedicated low-risk cleanup PR after beta, or never.

Phase 5's parallel-canary (compute locally AND fetch backend AND diff) is sometimes framed as a temporary cutover-window device — keeping it permanent is GOOD (see drift concern), but building elaborate per-design diff-reporting UI for it would be over-build; a dev-only console.warn on mismatch is sufficient.

Authoring 20-40 fixtures up front as a fixed target risks both over- and under-building: better to drive fixture count from a coverage floor (mechanical) than to hit an arbitrary number that feels complete but leaves branches uncovered. The number is a smell; the coverage gate is the substance.

Reimplementing Ed25519 bundle signing in TS is correctly scoped OUT (local mode has no signing key; signer==nil byte-identity path) — flagging it here only to AFFIRM that excluding it is right, not over-engineering; do not let scope-creep pull signing into local mode 'for completeness.'

other_smells.note: Line 63: `// eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` — the ONLY lint suppression in the whole frontend. Go side has zero `//nolint`. Leave the directive itself in place (behavior-bearing); just ensure any surrounding explanatory comment is English.

Clean signal: ZERO TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK markers in either Go non-test or frontend code. No commented-out code blocks found (apparent matches were all prose-continuation doc lines starting with 'for'/'if'). No debt to triage here.

`t.Errorf("默认路由不应再使用 `redistribute local`...")` — the ONLY place CJK appears outside a comment in real code. It is a test-failure diagnostic string (test-only, never user-facing), but should be Englishized in the same pass for consistency. All production error strings (errors.New/fmt.Errorf) are already English — verified zero CJK.

DO NOT translate the catalog VALUES (they are the legitimate Chinese UI translations). Only the 12 CJK section-header COMMENT lines (e.g. lines 105,148,185,566,578,587,591,596,616,620,624) get Englishized. en.ts is already all-English.
`中文` at line 21 is the visible UI label for the Chinese language option — NOT a comment. Must be left exactly as-is. This file has no CJK comments; exclude it from the comment-normalization pass.

10 .tsx files carry CJK in `{/* ... */}` JSX-comment form (LoginPage, CustomEdge, TopologyCanvas, DeployBar, EnrollmentFlow, NodeRegistry, EdgeEditor, BottomBar, NodeForm, SettingsPage). These are easy to miss with a `^\s*//` regex — the normalization tooling must also match `{/* */}` and block `/* */` forms, not just `//`.

Several CJK occurrences here and in wireguard.go/peers.go/model/topology.go/semantic.go are INLINE trailing comments on struct fields (e.g. script.go:12,77,78; wireguard.go:19,158). Confirmed NONE sit inside the backtick install.sh/wg/babel template literals — so translation is behavior-preserving, but reviewers must verify edits stay on the comment side of the field declaration.

Largest comment-translation surface: 267 CJK doc-comment lines + 15 trailing. semantic.go (213+18), topology.go (62+5), script.go (60+5), schema.go (63), babel.go (57). controllerStore.ts (308) and topologyStore.ts (106) are the FE hotspots. These four/five files are ~70% of the total work and warrant the most careful technical-meaning preservation (sticky-pin/keystone/custody invariants are documented in-comment here).

14 non-test Go files mix English godoc full sentences with CJK comments in the same file (server.go, handler.go, render.go, deploy.go, export.go, schema.go, semantic.go, compiler.go, peers.go, topology.go, babel.go, wireguard.go, script.go, ip.go). Result of a partial CJK-strip that left exported-symbol godoc as bare `// Name <leftover-noun>` stubs while leaving body comments in Chinese. Normalize to a single English godoc convention (`// Name <verb-phrase>.`).

hygiene.cleanup_plan:
NORMALIZATION STANDARD
1. Target language: ALL comments -> English. Preserve EXACT technical meaning — these comments document load-bearing invariants (sticky-pin allocation, served-vs-staged keystone split, zero-knowledge custody, mimic MTU-12, audit-dossier D-codes). Translate intent faithfully; do NOT paraphrase away precision or drop dossier refs (D6/D27-29/D36/D40/D53/D59/D78, plan-N tags, doc/spec citations) — keep those tokens verbatim.
2. Fix stubs into real godoc: every `// Name <leftover-noun>` becomes a proper godoc sentence beginning with the identifier and a verb, e.g. `// NewServer  API` -> `// NewServer constructs an HTTP API server with the given handler.`; `// cors CORS` -> `// cors wraps h with permissive CORS headers for the air-gap routes.` Recover meaning from the function body + the CODEBASE MAP, not from guesswork.
3. Comments ONLY — zero code/string/identifier edits. Confirmed safe: every CJK occurrence repo-wide is in a comment (// , /* */, {/* */}, or trailing field comment); NO CJK lives inside Go backtick templates or production error strings. Two non-comment exceptions are explicitly EXCLUDED from translation: zh.ts catalog VALUES (legit translations) and LanguageToggle.tsx `中文` (UI label). One test-only diagnostic string (babel_announce_test.go:166) MAY be Englishized but is not a comment.
4. Style: one godoc convention across the repo (sentence case, ends with a period, starts with the symbol name for exported decls). Keep inline trailing comments terse. Leave the single eslint-disable directive (CanvasToolbar.tsx:63) intact.

FILE ORDERING (lowest-risk leaf-first, build-verifiable in chunks)
Phase A — Go leaves + small files (low blast radius, fast to verify): internal/model/topology.go, internal/linkid (test only), internal/allocator/ip.go, internal/renderer/sysctl.go, internal/renderer/babel_presets.go, internal/validator/nat.go, internal/compiler/roles.go.
Phase B — Go mid-size renderers/validators: internal/renderer/{babel.go, wireguard.go, deploy.go}, internal/validator/schema.go, internal/artifacts/export.go, internal/api/server.go, internal/api/handler.go, internal/compiler/compiler.go, internal/render/render.go, cmd/compiler/main.go.
Phase C — Go god-files (highest care, most invariants): internal/compiler/peers.go (267+15), internal/validator/semantic.go (213+18), internal/renderer/script.go (60+5).
Phase D — Go test files (54 listed): mechanical, do last; lower risk since tests re-run as the gate.
Phase E — Frontend pure libs + types: lib/{uuid.ts, roleCapabilities.ts, compiledInterfaces.ts}, types/{topology.ts, controller.ts}, i18n/messages/zh.ts (comments only).
Phase F — Frontend components (incl. JSX {/* */} form): canvas/, deploy/, design/aside/, domains/, nodes/, auth/, audit/, layout/, pages/.
Phase G — Frontend stores (god-files, last): controllerStore.ts (308), topologyStore.ts (106).
Commit per phase (or per file for C/G) so a regression bisects to a small comment diff.

VERIFICATION GATE (run after EACH phase; full gate before final commit)
Go: `export PATH="$HOME/.local/go/bin:$PATH"` then `gofmt -l ./cmd ./internal` (must be empty — gofmt re-wraps long comment lines, so re-run after CJK->English since line widths change), `go build ./...`, `go vet ./...`, `go test ./...` (incl. internal/regression). Toolchain confirmed working: go1.26.4, GOPROXY mirror + GOSUMDB=off per memory; no current gofmt drift.
Frontend: `cd frontend && npm run build` (= `tsc -b && vite build`, the stricter project-references typecheck per project memory — NOT bare tsc --noEmit) and `npm run lint` (eslint .).
Because the change set is comments-only, ALL of build/vet/test/tsc output must be byte-for-byte equivalent in behavior; the air-gap byte-identity invariant (render/equivalence_test.go, script_signature_test.go, export_test.go) is the strongest tripwire — if any of those fail, a backtick template or a non-comment line was touched by mistake. CI (ci.yml) re-runs the same gofmt+vet+test+FE-build as the authoritative check on push.
hygiene.summary: Repo-wide comment-hygiene catalog. SCOPE: 19 Go non-test files + 35 Go test files (54 total of 180) and 34 frontend TS/TSX files carry CJK in comments. Every CJK occurrence repo-wide is in a COMMENT (//, /* */, {/* */}, trailing field comments) — verified NONE in Go backtick templates and NONE in production error strings (errors.New/fmt.Errorf are already English), so the entire normalization is behavior-preserving. STUBS: ~30 doc-comments were left as `// Name <leftover-noun>` by a partial CJK-strip (e.g. server.go:52 `// NewServer  API`, :123 `// cors CORS`, handler.go:31 `// Handler HTTP API`, ip.go:12 `// IPAllocator IP`, compiler.go:22 `// CompileResult`, sysctl.go:36 `// RenderSysctlConfig  sysctl`, plus FE topologyStore.ts:283 `// UI`, :286 `// Project`). DOC-STYLE INCONSISTENCY: 14 Go files mix English godoc with Chinese body comments. HOTSPOTS (~70% of work): peers.go (267 CJK comment lines), semantic.go (213), controllerStore.ts (308), topologyStore.ts (106), topology.go/babel.go/schema.go/script.go (~60 each). CLEAN SIGNALS: zero TODO/FIXME/HACK debt, no commented-out code, one eslint-disable (CanvasToolbar.tsx:63), zero Go //nolint. One test-only CJK diagnostic string at babel_announce_test.go:166. PLAN: translate all comments to English preserving exact technical meaning + dossier/plan/spec refs, fix stubs into real godoc sentences, order leaf-first (Go leaves -> renderers/validators -> god-files -> Go tests -> FE libs/types -> FE components incl. JSX {/* */} -> FE stores), and gate each phase with gofmt -l + go build/vet/test and `npm run build` (tsc -b) + eslint; the air-gap byte-identity tests (render/equivalence_test.go) are the tripwire that a non-comment line was touched.

frontend.topic: Frontend structure + Go↔TS model integrity (Zustand stores, controller API client, type drift, i18n)
frontend.structure_assessment: The frontend is well-organized and the typecheck is GREEN (npx tsc -b exits 0). Two Zustand stores split responsibility cleanly: topologyStore.ts (892 ln, the topology data SoT, persisted to localStorage) and controllerStore.ts (1637 ln, the controller-connection/auth/deploy SoT). The local-vs-server-authoritative split is the central axis and is implemented carefully: canvasFromServer marks a canvas as a server-held secret mirror, partialize (topologyStore.ts:856-880) blanks project/domains/nodes/edges from localStorage when (canvasFromServer && mode==='controller'), and the single clearServerCanvasAtGate predicate (controllerStore.ts:469) is reused at every session-flush point (logout, both checkSession session-loss branches, switchToLocal) so they cannot drift. The cross-store import cycle (topologyStore<->controllerStore) is resolved by discipline — lazy getState() inside actions, isDesignDirty kept a PURE exported fn (controllerStore.ts:207) to avoid eagerly resolving the TS type cycle — heavily documented but inherently fragile (a single eager top-level read reintroduces a real init-order cycle). Component layer: 46 .tsx files under feature folders (canvas/ design/aside/ deploy/ shell/ auth/ pages/ audit/ nodes/ domains/ layout/), thin/presentational, reading via the 3 zustand hooks. App.tsx uses react-router v7 with a RequireControllerMode deep-link guard (App.tsx:29) so controller-only routes redirect in local mode (reachability matches nav visibility). i18n is excellent: en.ts is canonical and DEFINES MessageKey = keyof typeof en, zh.ts is a Partial with per-key English fallback, a satisfies guard forces every UILanguage present, and EN/ZH have EXACT key parity (631 keys each, zero missing/dead — verified by diff). api/controllerClient.ts (1063 ln) is a clean per-route client with snake_case<->camelCase boundary mapping and a typed ControllerError carrying the coded error envelope for tError localization.
frontend.state_mgmt_notes: Three persisted stores (topology-storage, controller-storage, ui-storage), each with a hand-curated partialize allowlist that deliberately excludes all secrets (sessionToken/csrfToken/operatorToken never persisted; only NON-secret keystone identifiers credential_id/alg/rpId/public-key-PEM kept). Security-critical custody invariants are concentrated and well-implemented: stripPrivateKeys before every update-topology/deploy/save POST; dropAllKeys on controller import; an in-flight mode-flip guard in topologyStore.compile() (line 733) drops a result if mode flipped to controller mid-request so reconstructed private keys never land in the controller-mode store. Keystone status is SERVER-authoritative (serverOperatorPinned from GET /operator-credential, controllerStore ~315) — the browser-local operatorCredential* cache deliberately does NOT decide 'enrolled', closing the cleared-browser false-'Not enrolled' fleet-stranding footgun; re-pin is gated behind pendingKeystoneRotate + explicit ack. deploy() is a long imperative sequence (updateTopology->stage->GET trustlist->signManifest->postTrustlistSignature->promote->reconcile) with thorough best-effort error handling and a 3-way pin merge (mergeServerAllocations with baseEdges for non-clobber adoption). The dirty/conflict detection (canonicalDesign / canonicalDesignIgnoringPins) re-implements Go's encoding/json omitempty 'empty' semantics in JS (isEmptyVal at controllerStore.ts:128) — a correctness-sensitive mirror that must track the model's json tags by hand (PROJECT/DOMAIN/NODE/EDGE/PUBLIC_ENDPOINT_OMITEMPTY arrays). Notable structural risk: controllerStore is a 1637-line god-file mixing connection, auth (password/TOTP/passkey), keystone, deploy orchestration, saveDesign, hydration, and settings in one create() literal.

type_drift.issue: MISSING FIELD. Go Node has RouterID (Babel router-id, MAC-48). It is schema-validated (validator/schema.go:299-301 CodeNodeRouterIDInvalid), semantically warned for clients (semantic.go:436 CodeClientRouterIDMeaningless), and consumed by the babel renderer (renderer/babel.go:99). But the TS Node interface (types/topology.ts:34-58) has NO router_id field and NodeEditor.tsx offers no input. Both error-code messages ARE localized (en.ts:725, zh.ts:707), so the UI can DISPLAY a router_id validation error it can never have CAUSED via the UI. controllerStore NODE_OMITEMPTY (line 114) DOES list 'router_id', so a router_id in an imported/hydrated design survives the controller round-trip and canonical diff — but the typed model cannot represent it and the user cannot set it. Matters doubly for the planned TS compiler reimplementation: a browser compiler reading the TS Node type would drop router_id and regenerate one via GenerateRouterID, changing babeld config for any node with a pinned router-id.

ZERO-VALUE SEMANTICS (handled correctly, but load-bearing). is_enabled is the one Edge bool intentionally NON-omitempty on both sides, so it round-trips even when false. TS makes it required (good). All UI edge-creation paths set is_enabled:true explicitly (topologyStore.ts:390, TopologyCanvas.tsx:505). The Go-mirror helper correctly treats a MISSING is_enabled as DISABLED (normalizeEdges.ts:88 uses e.is_enabled !== true, with an explicit comment that === false would wrongly enable undefined). This is the subtlest Go-zero-value mirror in the codebase and is correct today — but a TS compiler reimplementation MUST preserve !== true (not truthiness) for any hand-edited/partial import.

MINOR/by-design. alloc_schema_version exists and round-trips in both (TS emits it only when >0). No field drift, but TS has no equivalent of CurrentAllocSchemaVersion — it never fails-closed on an INPUT topology stamped higher than it understands. A browser-compiler reimplementation would need to re-add this I10 guard or it would misread a future pin format as v1.

NO type drift in the core 4-slice model otherwise. Project, Domain (incl. transit_cidr), PublicEndpoint, NodeCapabilities, Edge (incl. all six pinned_* + role/transport/priority/weight/endpoint_host/endpoint_port/compiled_port), RoutePolicy, and the API response shapes (ValidateResponse/CompileResponse/CompileManifest) all match the Go json tags field-for-field. endpoint_port/compiled_port zero handling is consistent with Go omitempty throughout EdgeEditor (>0 checks, ?? undefined). route_policies is correctly reserved/stripped on both sides.

MAINTENANCE-COUPLING (a drift HAZARD, not a drift today): five hand-synced field lists enumerate model json tags with KEEP-IN-SYNC comments — ALLOCATION_PIN_FIELDS (topologyStore), PIN_FIELDS (normalizeEdges), and PROJECT/DOMAIN/NODE/EDGE/PUBLIC_ENDPOINT_OMITEMPTY (controllerStore). Any new model field silently drifts the omitempty-aware canonical diff (phantom save-conflicts or dropped pins). No automated check binds these to topology.go.

smells: GOD-FILES: controllerStore.ts (1637 ln) and controllerClient.ts (1063 ln) both well past the 800-line threshold. controllerStore bundles connection, password/TOTP/passkey auth, keystone enroll+sign, deploy orchestration, saveDesign, hydration, custody+shrink guards, and settings into one create() literal.

AUTH-HANDLING BUG (controllerClient.ts:971-985, getTrustlist): the ONLY authenticated route that bypasses the shared request() helper. It builds a raw fetch with Authorization: Bearer ${cfg.operatorToken} and NO credentials:'include' and NO CSRF. After a page refresh the in-memory sessionToken is gone, so configOf yields an EMPTY operatorToken for a cookie-only session; getTrustlist then sends 'Bearer ' (empty) with no cookie -> 401. GET /trustlist IS operatorAuth-wrapped server-side (handler_controller.go:266) and operatorAuth accepts cookie+CSRF. So a refreshed cookie-session operator's Deploy keystone-signing step (controllerStore.ts:1298) fails on a keystone-ON tenant. login/passkey-begin/finish omit credentials by design (unauth) and GET /session correctly includes them — getTrustlist is the lone defect.

CROSS-STORE IMPORT CYCLE held together only by discipline (lazy getState(), pure isDesignDirty) — a single eager top-level cross-store read reintroduces a real module-init cycle. Documented heavily but unenforced.

DEAD DEPENDENCY: zod v4 is in package.json but grep -r zod src finds zero imports — the client trusts all server JSON via 'as' casts with no runtime validation. Also unreferenced: src/assets/{hero.png,react.svg,vite.svg}.

TYPE-CAST ESCAPE HATCHES: mergeServerAllocations (topologyStore.ts:417-419) and stripPins (normalizeEdges.ts:68) route Edge through 'as unknown as Record<string,unknown>' to do dynamic field copy/delete on the EXACT pin fields hand-synced to Go tags — defeating type-checking precisely where the model-mirror risk is highest.

LAYER LEAK: 5 deploy components (AgentUpdateSettings, BootstrapSettings, MimicCatalogSettings, TwoFactorSettings, UpdateStatusChip) import api/controllerClient DIRECTLY instead of through the store — inconsistent boundary vs the rest of the controller flows.

NO FRONTEND TESTS: zero *.test/*.spec, no test runner in package.json, CI frontend job is lint+build only. The Go-mirroring pure helpers (healCollidingPins, deriveUpdateState, canonicalDesign, custody) are explicitly authored 'so a future FE test runner can cover it' but nothing runs them — the Go↔TS mirror is verified only by manual/backend parity.

MIXED-LANGUAGE COMMENTS: large swaths of both stores, controllerClient, and several lib files are commented in Chinese while others are English (often interleaved within one file), matching the Go-side smell.

OVERLOADED 'compile' VERB across stores: topologyStore.compile() (air-gap, real keys) vs controllerStore.compilePreview() (server, placeholder keys) vs deploy() (stage/promote) — easy to conflate; no naming hint distinguishes them.
recommendations: FIX getTrustlist to use the shared request() helper (or at minimum add credentials:'include' + the X-CSRF-Token/Bearer pair). As written it breaks the Deploy keystone-signing flow for any operator whose session survives only via the httpOnly cookie after a refresh. This is the one concrete correctness bug found and it sits on the security-critical deploy path. Repro: log in, refresh (in-memory sessionToken cleared, cookie session intact), Deploy on a keystone-ON tenant -> GET /trustlist 401.

Add the missing router_id field to the TS Node interface (types/topology.ts) and a NodeEditor input gated like RouterID validation (optional; MAC-48 or IPv4), OR explicitly document it as deliberately-unsupported-in-UI. Today the UI can localize a router_id validation error it can never trigger, and a future browser-compiler reimplementation would silently drop a pinned router-id. controllerStore already preserves it on round-trip, so the field is half-supported.

Before the TS compiler reimplementation: treat the hand-synced field lists (ALLOCATION_PIN_FIELDS, PIN_FIELDS, the *_OMITEMPTY arrays) and the Go-zero-value mirrors (is_enabled !== true, isEmptyVal's omitempty definition, normalizeEdges.linkKey/canonicalIP, the missing CurrentAllocSchemaVersion guard) as the load-bearing contract. Generate the TS model + omitempty metadata from internal/model/topology.go (codegen) so a Go tag change cannot silently drift the canonical diff, rather than relying on KEEP-IN-SYNC comments.

Adopt zod (already a dependency, currently dead) to validate server JSON at the controllerClient boundary instead of 'as' casts — it would also catch wire-shape drift at runtime, exactly the Go↔TS class of bug this review targets.

Split controllerStore.ts and controllerClient.ts along their natural seams (auth/session, keystone, deploy/save, settings) into smaller slices/modules — the 1637-line create() literal is the region's primary structural maintenance risk.

Add a minimal FE test runner (vitest) and cover the pure Go-mirror helpers (healCollidingPins, canonicalDesign/isEmptyVal, custody dropAllKeys/stripPrivateKeys, deriveUpdateState) with cases that pin the Go-zero-value semantics — these are the functions most likely to silently break on a backend model change and currently have zero automated coverage.

phone_readiness.summary: Not a phone/mobile-readiness task — this is a desktop operator panel (React Flow canvas topology editor + fleet dashboard). No mobile-specific work was in scope or assessed; reporting structural/type-drift findings instead.

frontend.structure_assessment:
The panel is a desktop-only layout with effectively NO responsive design. The app-shell is a fixed two-column flex row: `Shell.tsx:81` is `flex h-screen`, with `Sidebar.tsx:24-27` hardcoded to `w-16` (collapsed) / `w-60` (expanded) and `shrink-0`, sitting permanently beside the routed content. There is no mobile drawer, no overlay, no breakpoint that hides or floats the sidebar — on a 360px phone an expanded sidebar consumes two-thirds of the width and there is no way to reclaim it short of the icon-collapse toggle (still 64px). A repo-wide grep found Tailwind responsive prefixes (sm:/md:/lg:/xl:) in only 3 of ~50 component files (AuditView.tsx:136, EnrollmentFlow.tsx:96-122, ConnectionSettings.tsx:25) and every occurrence is just `md:grid-cols-*` — there is zero `sm:`/`lg:` usage and no JS-driven responsiveness (the only matchMedia is ThemeProvider's dark-mode query). So the operator surfaces were authored at one fixed (desktop) width.

The Design route is the worst offender for narrow viewports but is explicitly desktop-first per the brief: DesignPage.tsx stacks three `shrink-0` columns — the global sidebar + a `w-72` lists drawer (DesignPage.tsx:27) + the canvas + a `w-80` selection aside (DesignAside.tsx:18) — with no responsive collapse on any of them, plus a fixed `h-40` validation footer. The React Flow canvas itself (TopologyCanvas.tsx:565) keeps default touch handling so pinch-zoom/pan work, but the Controls/MiniMap and `min-w-[120px]` node cards are tiny on a phone. Keeping the canvas desktop-first is reasonable; the blocker is that the canvas's neighboring chrome doesn't yield.

The genuinely phone-TARGETED operator surfaces (Fleet, Deploy, Nodes, Audit, Overview, Settings) are all single-column scroll pages (`h-full overflow-y-auto p-6`) which is the right base shape, but they were never adapted: fixed `p-6` padding eats ~48px of a phone's width; OverviewPage.tsx:33,42 uses `grid-cols-3` that never collapses to one column; the NodeRegistry is an 8-column `<table>` (NodeRegistry.tsx:77-88) — it is wrapped in `overflow-x-auto` so it scrolls rather than breaks the layout, but it is cramped and not card-reflowed for phones. The good news: the LoginPage (LoginPage.tsx:67-68, `max-w-sm` centered single column) is already mobile-clean, the `index.html:6` viewport meta is correct, modal dialogs use `p-4`+`max-w-md` (fit small screens), and tap targets are mostly acceptable (sidebar/nav items are `h-10`, login/deploy buttons `py-2`/`py-1.5`); the sub-par tap targets are the small `px-2 py-1 text-xs` table-action buttons (Revoke/Cancel rekey in NodeRegistry) and the `px-2.5 py-1 text-xs` Topbar I/O buttons, which fall under the ~44px touch-target guideline.</structure_assessment>

recommendations: Convert the sidebar to a responsive off-canvas drawer: below a breakpoint (e.g. `lg`), hide the docked `aside` and render it as a fixed overlay/slide-in toggled by a hamburger button added to the Topbar (Topbar.tsx). Above the breakpoint keep the current `w-16/w-60` docked behavior. This single change unlocks usable width on every operator page and is the highest-leverage fix.

Verify with `npm run build` (tsc -b, the stricter project-references typecheck) after the changes, per the project's recorded CI gotcha, and smoke-test the shell drawer + Fleet/Deploy at ~360px and ~768px widths in browser devtools; there is no frontend test runner so this is manual.

Scope the work as a single 'responsive operator surfaces' plan: shell drawer first (unblocks everything), then the per-page breakpoint pass + NodeRegistry card reflow + tap-target bumps; explicitly carve the React Flow design canvas out of scope as desktop-first to match the owner's stated target.
smells: Near-total absence of responsive CSS: only 3/~50 frontend files use any Tailwind breakpoint prefix and all are `md:grid-cols-*` (AuditView.tsx:136, EnrollmentFlow.tsx:96-122, ConnectionSettings.tsx:25). The shell and all pages were authored at one fixed desktop width.
