No frontend test runner exists (no *.test/*.spec, no vitest), so any responsive regression would only be caught by manual browser testing.

compiler.determinism_assessment:
The allocation core is genuinely deterministic and order-independent BY CONSTRUCTION, and the recent cross-subgraph pin-collision fix is sound. VALUES (overlay IP, listen port, transit-IP pair, link-local pair) are reproducible regardless of array order:

- IP allocation (allocator/ip.go): clears out-of-CIDR stale IPs, marks all surviving IPs used, then fills gaps by ascending host index. Stable per node ID; uses uint32 arithmetic with explicit network/broadcast/host-bit/overflow guards. No map iteration affects values.
- Per-peer allocation (peers.go derivePeersWithDomains): the "reserve-all-pins-first, then gap-fill" design is the right one. Pass 1 phase 3 reserves every complete pin pair; phase 4 sorts unpinned links by linkKey (peers.go:484) and picks the lowest free slot per pool. Because the reserved set a link sees depends only on (current pins + smaller-linkKey unpinned links), VALUES are a pure function of link identity and the current set — satisfying I2/I3/I9 (order independence, identity binding, delete/re-add idempotence). I confirmed sort.Slice(links, linkKey) at peers.go:484 is the ONLY allocation-order sort, and it is keyed on identity, not array position.
- linkid.LinkKey/PinKey/IsBackup correctly fold primary-class edges (both directions + accidental same-direction duplicates) into one link and discriminate each backup by edge ID — so parallel/backup links never share an allocation, and adding/removing a backup never re-keys another link.
- The cross-subgraph reservation (BuildReservedFromExcludedEdges, peers.go:61) correctly reserves out-of-subgraph edges' pins into BOTH endpoint domains' transit pools (peers.go:103-110), skips disabled/client/partial-pin edges consistently with the allocator and validator, and is wired only on the controller subgraph path (compile.go:159). Full compiles pass reserved=nil and are byte-unchanged. normalize.HealCollidingPins mirrors the validator's dedup exactly (same linkKey grouping, same canonicalIP, same client/disabled skips, two-phase claim/strip). The prevent (reserve) vs clean (heal) split is coherent.

Map iteration: there IS map iteration in render.All (ClientConfigs, render.go:193) and both renderers (peerMap, wireguard.go:184 / babel.go:192), but each writes into a per-nodeID map ENTRY, never concatenating across nodes, so cross-node output is iteration-order-independent. The bundle digest is iteration-safe: bundlesig.Canonicalize sorts file paths by byte order (bundlesig.go:174-179), so checksums.sha256 (and therefore the keystone bundleSHA256) is deterministic.

THE ONE REAL DETERMINISM GAP: PeerInfo VALUES are order-independent, but the ORDER of PeerInfo within a node's slice is NOT — Pass 2 (peers.go:570) appends peers in topo.Edges array-iteration order, not sorted by linkKey. The WireGuard renderer is immune (one file per interface). But the Babel renderer concatenates peers into a single babeld.conf in slice order (babel.go:118-134 interface lines, babel.go:167-171 client /32 redistribute lines), so reordering the edges array reorders those lines and changes the babeld.conf bytes for an otherwise-unchanged node. This violates the I1/I8 byte-stability + I2 order-independence guarantee for the babel artifact specifically (and thus that file's hash / the node's bundle digest), even though all allocated values are identical.

correctness_findings.title: Babel config line order depends on edge-array position, not link identity — breaks I2/I8 byte-stability for babeld.conf
correctness_findings.detail: Allocated VALUES are order-independent (gap-fill is sorted by linkKey at peers.go:484), but PeerInfo are appended into peerMap[nodeID] in topo.Edges array-iteration order (peers.go:570 Pass 2 loop is `for i := range topo.Edges`). The Babel renderer concatenates that slice into a single babeld.conf: interface lines at babel.go:118-134 and client-/32 redistribute lines at babel.go:167-171 are emitted in slice order. So two topologies equal as SETS but with edges in different array order produce byte-different babeld.conf (and therefore a different file hash / bundle digest) for an unchanged node — exactly the I2 order-independence / I8 byte-determinism violation the allocation spec forbids (allocation-stability.md I2, I8). The WireGuard renderer is immune because it writes one file per interface (wireguard.go:202 key=nodeID:interfaceName). Fix: sort each node's peer slice by a stable key (InterfaceName, or the link identity) before rendering, or sort Interfaces/redistribute prefixes inside babel.go. This is the single most important byte-exact hazard for a TS reimplementation: the conformance harness will flag babeld.conf diffs on edge reorder unless both sides agree on the (currently incidental) ordering.

correctness_findings.title: Re-enabling a disabled edge can collide with a link that gap-filled into its freed slot (compile-time error, not silent — but a stranding footgun)
correctness_findings.detail: In a FULL compile, Pass 1 only builds linkEntity for IsEnabled edges (peers.go:351-355), so a DISABLED edge's pins are NOT reserved (correct per I9 — disabling frees the slot). The validator (semantic.go:557) and HealCollidingPins (normalize/pins.go:75) also skip disabled edges. Scenario: disable edge A-B (its pins persist on the edge), add edge A-C which gap-fills into A-B's now-free transit pair / port / link-local and gets that value written back as its pin. Later RE-ENABLE A-B: now both A-B (old pin) and A-C (new pin) are enabled with the SAME pinned values but DIFFERENT link identities. On the next compile this is caught by validateAllocationPins (CodePin*DuplicateCrossLink) so it FAILS the compile rather than emitting wrong config — good — but it is an operator-facing surprise (re-enabling an untouched link bricks the compile) and the heal won't auto-repair it because it strips the LATER edge in slice order, which may be A-B (the one the operator wants back) rather than A-C. Worth a targeted test + possibly auto-heal-on-reenable. Not wrong-but-valid output, but an availability/UX edge case in the disabled→reenable path.

correctness_findings.title: Reverse-endpoint fallback keys on operator-supplied HasPublicIP, not on presence of public endpoints — can silently omit a dialable Endpoint
correctness_findings.detail: peers.go:783 gates the reverse-peer endpoint fallback on `fromNode.Capabilities.HasPublicIP && len(fromNode.PublicEndpoints) > 0`. HasPublicIP is a raw operator-supplied model field (model/topology.go:126); InferCapabilitiesFromRole PRESERVES it (roles.go:109 `caps := node.Capabilities`) and never derives it from PublicEndpoints. So a node that genuinely has a public endpoint configured but whose has_public_ip checkbox is false produces a reverse peer with an EMPTY Endpoint — a valid-looking config where the reverse side can never dial out and the tunnel only forms if the other side initiates. This is a wrong-but-valid output: it compiles clean (the NAT validator only WARNS, nat.go:51-52), renders a peer with no Endpoint line, and the operator gets a silently one-directional link. Consider deriving/normalizing HasPublicIP from len(PublicEndpoints)>0 during capability inference, or warning when a node has public_endpoints but has_public_ip=false. Also note PublicEndpoints[0] is used (peers.go:785) — the FIRST endpoint by array order, which is deterministic but arbitrary if multiple are configured.

correctness_findings.title: computeChecksum uses fmt.Sprintf("%v", topo) over the struct — not a stable canonical encoding
correctness_findings.detail: compiler.go:234-238 computes the manifest Checksum as sha256(fmt.Sprintf("%v", topo))[:16]. fmt %v over a struct containing slices is positionally stable but is NOT a canonical encoding (it is sensitive to slice order and Go's fmt formatting, and would differ from any TS reimplementation). It is only a display digest in CompileManifest.Checksum (not the keystone/bundle digest, which correctly uses bundlesig.Canonicalize over sorted file paths). Low impact today, but it must NOT be relied upon for equivalence, and a TS port cannot reproduce it byte-for-byte — flag it as display-only / exclude from conformance.

correctness_findings.title: Duplicated defaultTransitCIDR / backupDefaultLinkCost constants kept in sync only by comment (drift risk)
correctness_findings.detail: defaultTransitCIDR="10.10.0.0/24" is defined independently in compiler/peers.go:21, validator/semantic.go:499, and renderer/script.go:805; backupDefaultLinkCost=384 in compiler/peers.go:137 and validator/semantic.go:798; minPinnedPort=1024 and the 51820 base also straddle compiler+validator. Comments assert they MUST agree but nothing enforces it. If the validator's defaultTransitCIDR drifted from the compiler's, the validator would pass a pin the compiler then rejects (or vice versa) — a validator-completeness gap. deploy.go additionally HARDCODES 10.10.0.0/24 SNAT literals (deploy.go ~310/370/482/568) that ignore a custom domain transit_cidr entirely, so a non-default transit CIDR yields a deploy script whose SNAT cleanup targets the wrong pool. For a TS port these shared constants should be single-sourced.

correctness_findings.title: Validator completeness vs allocator: link-local pool exhaustion is unreachable in practice but transit/port pin out-of-pool checks are complete
correctness_findings.detail: Cross-checked validator vs allocator: validateAllocationPins covers pair-completeness, port range [1024,65535], transit-in-CIDR, and cross-link dedup; effective-port-range overflow is checked (semantic.go:295). The allocator's gapFillTransitPair correctly bounds to (2^hostBits-2)/2 pairs and rejects network/broadcast (peers.go:875-879), surfacing CodeTransitPoolExhausted before any illegal address — matching I6. One asymmetry: gapFillLinkLocalPair (peers.go:956) has an UNBOUNDED for-loop with no exhaustion guard, justified because fe80::/10 is effectively infinite, but a hostile/huge topology that somehow consumed the low link-local indices could spin; bounded in practice by maxTopologyEdges=10000 so not exploitable. No validator gap that produces wrong-but-valid output was found in this area — the pin validation runs BEFORE reservation (I7) as required.

edge_cases: Multi-CIDR transit pools: HANDLED. transitCIDRForNode resolves each link's pool from its from-node domain (peers.go:27), usedTransitIPs is keyed per-CIDR, and cross-subgraph reservation reserves into both endpoints' pools (peers.go:103-110). A transit IP physically lives in one pool only, so reserving in the other is a harmless no-op. DeriveClientConfigs unions all domain CIDRs + per-domain transit CIDRs deterministically by topo.Domains order with dedup (peers.go:1172-1190).

Parallel links: HANDLED. Each backup edge is its own link identity (linkid.LinkKey appends #edgeID); primary-class edges fold to one link. Backup interface names diverge via sha256(remoteName|edgeID)[:4] (naming.go WgInterfaceNameForEdge). validateSinglePrimaryPerPair + validateParallelLinkCosts + validateInterfaceNameUniqueness guard the parallel-link semantics.

Backup links: HANDLED for allocation/identity, but cost-derivation order (deriveLinkCost: Priority>0 then Weight>0 then backup-384 then 0) is mirrored in the validator (effectiveLinkCost). Equal-cost parallel links only WARN, not error.

Role flips (e.g. primary->backup, or node role to/from client): the link IDENTITY changes when role flips to backup (LinkKey gains #edgeID), which intentionally re-keys it to a fresh gap-fill. A role flip that orphans the old pinned values is the corruption HealCollidingPins / the disabled-reenable finding above addresses. Worth a conformance test on role flip specifically.
Disabled edges: pins are NOT reserved in full compile (correct I9 freeing) and NOT validated/healed while disabled — see the re-enable collision finding (medium).

Client edges: HANDLED. Client uses single wg0, no per-peer port/transit reservation; the non-client side still gets a listen port; validator rejects client port pins (CodePinClientPortPin) and warns on ignored transit/LL pins. DeriveClientConfigs picks the FIRST enabled outbound edge (peers.go:1121) — deterministic because the validator enforces exactly one.

Incremental enrollment ordering: HANDLED by the reservation pass. Each enrollment compiles a different subgraph; BuildReservedFromExcludedEdges makes the subgraph allocate around out-of-subgraph pins so two never-co-compiled edges don't collide. Existing corruption cleaned by heal on save/deploy/load.
IPv6 link-local formatting uses %x (hex), pair N -> fe80::(2N+1), fe80::(2N+2) (peers.go:1007). A TS port MUST use hex, not decimal (the D70 footgun the code comment calls out).
Endpoint formatting: hand-rolled itoa/formatEndpoint (peers.go:1030-1056) with IPv6 bracket detection by scanning for ':'. A TS port must reproduce bracketing of IPv6 hosts exactly.

portability_to_ts.note: Pure uint32 arithmetic over IPv4 CIDR with reserved-range handling. The only subtlety is net.ParseCIDR/net.IP.Contains semantics and To4() normalization — TS needs an IPv4 parser that matches Go's canonicalization (e.g. trailing-zero, leading-zero handling). No external deps.

Logic is pure (sort by linkKey string, lowest-free-slot per pool). The byte-exact hazards: (1) string comparison for linkKey sort MUST be byte-wise like Go (peers.go:484) — JS default sort is UTF-16; use explicit byte/codepoint compare; (2) reproduce the PeerInfo slice APPEND order (currently edge-array order) OR fix it to a sorted order on both sides — see the babel determinism finding; (3) hex link-local formatting; (4) the from/to direction mirroring on pin write-back (compiler.go:177-208) is intricate and must match exactly.
Trivial string ops; only watch byte-wise min/max of node IDs (PinKey uses Go string <= which is byte-wise).

Large surface (~20 sub-validators) but mostly pure string/regex/CIDR checks. Regexes (nodeNameCharset etc.) and the ~90 stable CodeXxx error codes must port verbatim — they are an API-visible contract. canonicalIP relies on net.ParseIP normalization which TS must match for dedup to agree with the heal.

Deterministic but byte-exact: lowercase + rune-map (interface cleaner drops '_', installer keeps it), multi-hyphen collapse, 15-char limit with sha256(remoteName)[:4] hash suffix on the long path, and sha256(remoteName+'|'+edgeID)[:4] for backups. TS needs SHA-256 (crypto.subtle/Web Crypto) and must slice clean[:8] with the same byte/codepoint semantics; multi-byte names could diverge on slicing.

Pure two-phase claim/strip over edges; mirrors validator dedup. A browser mirror already exists (frontend lib/normalizeEdges.ts) — the risk is the two drifting, since both hand-mirror the Go linkKey + canonicalIP rules.
Pure switch tables. Note HasPublicIP is preserved from input (not derived), which the TS port must replicate to stay byte-identical (and which is itself a finding).

THE blocker. wgtypes.GeneratePrivateKey is Curve25519 clamped key generation; deriving public from private is X25519 base-point mult. Reproducible in TS via a WASM/JS X25519 lib, but byte-exact key MATERIAL can never be conformance-tested (keys are random); only the derive-public-from-private and parse/format-base64 paths can. Air-gap byte-identity for KEY lines is impossible to assert deterministically — the conformance harness must inject fixed keys.

Go text/template output is byte-sensitive (whitespace, trailing newlines, conditional blocks). The script.go templates are huge embedded bash; reproducing them in TS is a large but mechanical transcription. The babel.go peer-ordering issue (finding) must be resolved before a TS babeld.conf can match. script.go sort.Ints(ports) for mimic ports is already deterministic.

compiler.summary:
The compiler's allocation core is well-designed and genuinely deterministic at the VALUE level: the reserve-all-pins-first then linkKey-sorted gap-fill (peers.go:484) makes overlay IPs, ports, transit pairs, and link-locals a pure function of link identity and the current pin set, satisfying the spec's I2/I3/I9 order-independence and idempotence invariants by construction. The recent cross-subgraph pin-collision fix (BuildReservedFromExcludedEdges reserve + normalize.HealCollidingPins clean) is coherent and correctly handles multi-CIDR pools, parallel/backup links, client edges, and incremental enrollment. The keystone bundle digest is iteration-safe (bundlesig sorts file paths). Tests are green.

The one HIGH finding is a residual byte-stability gap: PeerInfo VALUES are order-independent but their SLICE ORDER is edge-array-position-dependent (peers.go:570 iterates topo.Edges by index), and the Babel renderer concatenates that slice into a single babeld.conf (babel.go:118-134, 167-171) — so reordering edges changes babeld.conf bytes for an unchanged node, violating I2/I8 for that artifact. The WireGuard renderer is immune (one file per interface). Two MEDIUM findings: (a) re-enabling a disabled edge can collide with a link that gap-filled into its freed slot (caught as a compile error, not silent corruption, but a stranding/auto-heal-doesn't-help footgun); (b) the reverse-endpoint fallback keys on operator-supplied HasPublicIP rather than presence of public endpoints, which can silently emit a one-directional link (wrong-but-valid). LOW: shared constants (defaultTransitCIDR x3, backupDefaultLinkCost x2) kept in sync by comment only, deploy.go hardcodes 10.10.0.0/24 SNAT, and computeChecksum uses non-canonical fmt %v.

For a TS reimplementation: the allocator, linkid, validator, naming, normalize, and role-semantics layers are pure-stdlib and port easily-to-moderately (watch byte-wise string sort for linkKey, hex link-local formatting, net.ParseIP canonicalization, and SHA-256 for interface-name hashing). The ONLY hard blocker is wgtypes Curve25519 keygen (render.go) — byte-exact key material can't be conformance-tested, so the harness must inject fixed keys. The babel peer-ordering issue should be fixed (sort peers by a stable key) before any TS babeld.conf can be byte-conformant. Key files: internal/compiler/peers.go, compiler.go, internal/allocator/ip.go, internal/validator/semantic.go + schema.go, internal/linkid/linkid.go, internal/normalize/pins.go, internal/naming/naming.go, internal/renderer/babel.go, internal/render/render.go, internal/controller/compile.go.

tests_ci.coverage_summary:
OVERALL: The Go suite is heavyweight and genuinely load-bearing. 109 test files, ~26.8k test LOC vs ~23.4k non-test internal+cmd LOC (~1.15:1). Full `go test ./...` runs in well under a second when cached; a fresh `-race` run of the concurrency-heavy trio (regression 1.2s, controller 5.0s, api 11.6s) is ~14s and PASSES clean. gofmt drift, `go vet ./...`, and frontend eslint config are all clean locally.

PER-PACKAGE COVERAGE (go test -cover): linkid 100%, naming 97.4%, normalize 96.4%, allocator 91.9%, validator 91.6%, apierr 90.6%, trustlist 90.5%, bundlesig 89.6%, compiler 86.7%, renderer 86.6%, render 84.1%, controller 82.9%, artifacts 75.9%, agent 69.8%, api 69.1%; cmd/server, cmd/compiler, cmd/agent all 0.0%.

WELL-TESTED (high confidence for rc.1):
- The keystone/membership trust path. internal/regression/keystone_regression_test.go (566 ln, 9 adversarial black-box scenarios) drives the REAL exported surfaces end-to-end over MemStore: mixed-fleet rotation (node still pinned to A fail-closed-refuses a B-signed bundle), equal-epoch-across-rotation (the `<` vs `<=` regression guard at a POSITIVE epoch), anti-rollback rejects strictly-older epoch, algorithm-confusion (ES256 pin refuses Ed25519 artifact), bundle-signing-anchor x keystone composition, revoke-updates-membership, and the two beta.5 bug repros: re-stage-does-not-brick-served-config (#1) and GetServedConfig-atomic-under-concurrent-promote (#9, a real goroutine reader hammered while the writer promotes 12 membership-changing deploys, asserting no torn old-bundle/new-manifest pair; correctly designed to run under -race with the reader using only t.Errorf). No t.Skip / testing.Short gate — it always runs in CI. This is the single highest-value test asset in the repo.
- Self-update SAFETY DECISION logic (the brick-risk surface) is excellently covered despite the package's 69.8% headline: compareVersions 100%, decideSelfUpdate 100%, isForced 100%, parseAgentCatalog 100%, and the boot reconcile state machine ReconcileSelfUpdateEarly 87.5% / Promote 82.4% / FinalizeSelfUpdate 87.5% / rollbackAndAbandon 95.5% / performSelfUpdate 74.5%. The agent's headline number is dragged down by host-mutating I/O glue (renameOrCopy 7.7%, source.go HTTPSource/Fetch/Report/NewSourceFromSpec 0%) that is inherently network/filesystem-bound, not by the security logic.
- Agent<->controller WIRE CONTRACT is exercised END-TO-END: internal/agent/controller_client_test.go and keystone_test.go spin a real httptest.Server wired to internal/api + internal/controller and drive enroll/config/poll/rekey/report, so the deliberately-duplicated private wire structs (agent re-declaring server JSON) are caught if they drift.
- Store dual-impl parity: controller/store_compat_test.go (1441 ln) runs the same suite against MemStore AND FileStore, the only guard keeping the two implementations in lockstep.
- HTTP controller surface: api/controller_http_test.go (1193 ln) plus dedicated airgap_auth_gate, controller_keystone, keystone_repro, keystone_rotation, cookie_session, handler_login/passkey/totp, release_pins, server_limits, topology_custody, i18n_gate suites. The air-gap byte-identity invariant is pinned by render/equivalence_test.go + renderer/script_signature_test.go + artifacts/export_test.go.
- Compiler pin logic: allocation_stability_test.go (574 ln) + peers_endpoint_test.go (453 ln) + reserved_test.go guard the sticky-pin/reservation/collision rule.
untested_areas: FRONTEND HAS ZERO AUTOMATED TESTS. No vitest/jest, no *.test/*.spec files anywhere in frontend/src, no `test` script in frontend/package.json. The CI `frontend` job is lint + build (tsc -b) only. This leaves the Go-mirroring pure helpers entirely unverified by any runner: lib/normalizeEdges.ts healCollidingPins (mirrors Go normalize.HealCollidingPins + linkid.LinkKey), lib/updateStatus.ts deriveUpdateState (markers quoted verbatim from Go selfupdate.go), lib/custody.ts stripPrivateKeys/dropAllKeys (the zero-knowledge custody guard), lib/webauthn.ts (byte-contract mirror of Go internal/trustlist/webauthn.go), and the controllerStore deploy orchestration (stage->sign->promote) + canvasFromServer secret-flush invariant. Any backend model/json-tag/marker change silently breaks these mirrors with no test gate.

cmd/* mains are 0% covered. cmd/server/main.go (env-gated air-gap-vs-controller mode select, two-port serve, SIGTERM graceful drain), cmd/server/operator.go (create-operator argon2 account write), and cmd/agent/main.go (603-line daemon loop + the self-update reconcile PhaseA/PhaseB/Finalize call-ORDER sequencing — the documented load-bearing invariant) have no *_test.go. The reconcile ordering and graceful-shutdown wiring are exercised only indirectly via package-level unit tests, never at the main package where the call sites actually live.

Server lifecycle wiring in internal/api is uncovered: ListenAndServeAgent 0%, AgentHandler 0%, EnableStatic 0%, static.go spaHandler indirectly. These are only reachable from the untested cmd/server main, so the two-mux/two-port boot path is effectively untested above the handler level.

Host-side self-update I/O glue: renameOrCopy 7.7% (the cross-filesystem fallback for the binary swap — the literal moment a bad write could brick the agent), copyFile 50%, downloadTo 64.3%. The decision to swap is fully tested; the mechanics of the swap on a real filesystem are thin. selfupdate uses syscall.Exec (indirected via execFn) — inherently host-only, can't be browser/unit covered, but the rename/copy fallback could be.

Minor handler gaps: HandleTOTPDisable 0%, loginratelimit.pruneLocked 0% (GC path), validateAgentRollout 35.7%, release_pins.newReleasePinClient 37.5% (the SSRF-guard happy path is only testable via the injected releaseClient field, by design).

No automated DOC-LINK or doc-vs-code gate: 5 docs/spec files link to an archived implementation_plans path (broken), and repository-structure.md is badly stale (8 of 17 internal packages undocumented). CI has no link-check. Not a release blocker but unguarded drift.
tests_ci.ci_assessment:
.github/workflows/ci.yml gates every PR and push-to-main with two jobs: (1) go = gofmt-drift check on ./cmd ./internal + `go vet ./...` + `go test ./...` (so internal/regression runs like any package); (2) frontend = npm ci --legacy-peer-deps + `npm run lint` + `npm run build`. Both use go-version-file: go.mod and node 20. This is a solid, correct merge gate and it is green locally.

The `npm run build` = `tsc -b && vite build` choice is RIGHT and load-bearing: tsc -b uses project references (tsconfig.app.json: strict + noUnusedLocals/Params + verbatimModuleSyntax + erasableSyntaxOnly) which is stricter than a bare `tsc --noEmit` against the loose root config — per project memory, CI once caught a TS2352 the loose config missed. Keep build (not --noEmit) as the FE gate. tsc -b is the de-facto FE type-test stand-in until a real runner exists.

GAPS / RISKS in CI:
- NO -race in CI. The suite passes -race locally and the concurrency-critical regression scenario #9 + the controller tenantlock/FileStore-mutex paths exist specifically to be raced. Without -race in CI, a future concurrency regression in GetServedConfig atomicity or the served/staged split would not be caught at merge. HIGHEST-VALUE CI ADD.
- NO -count or coverage floor. Cache can mask a newly-flaky test; there is no coverage-regression gate so a PR can silently drop a package from (say) 90% to 40%.
- FLAKINESS: none observed. Zero t.Parallel() in the suite, and 6 packages use t.Setenv (bundlesig, controller, render x2, regression, artifacts) for YAOG_BUNDLE_SIGNING_KEY which forces those tests serial — so there is no env-global cross-test contamination risk, at the cost of no intra-package parallelism. Clean but not fast-by-parallelism.
- LOCAL-DEV quirk (not a CI issue): `go test ./...` locally picks up frontend/node_modules/flatted/golang/pkg/flatted as a Go package. node_modules is gitignored (frontend/.gitignore:10) so it is absent in CI checkout — harmless in CI, mildly confusing locally. Could add `GOFLAGS` or a skip, but not required.
tests_ci.release_pipeline_notes:
release.yml (on tag v*): re-gates with gate-go (vet + test) and gate-frontend (lint), then build-frontend (npm run build, uploads dist artifact), then build-bundles (7-target matrix: linux amd64/arm64/386/armv7 + windows amd64/arm64/386) building yaog-server/compiler/agent with CGO_ENABLED=0 and -ldflags "-s -w -X main.BuildVersion=<tag>" (tag passed via env BUILD_VERSION, not inline ${{ }}, to keep it out of shell-parse), packages tar.gz/zip bundles + standalone per-arch yaog-agent + a .sha256 sidecar, then release via softprops/action-gh-release@v2. fail-fast:false on the matrix so one arch failing doesn't kill the rest. The standalone-agent .sha256 sidecar is correctly documented as a pin-FILLING convenience, NOT a trust anchor (the agent always verifies self-update against the SIGNED in-bundle artifacts.json pin).

docker.yml (on tag v* or workflow_dispatch): multi-arch (linux/amd64,arm64) controller image to GHCR (always, via GITHUB_TOKEN) + Docker Hub (conditional on DOCKERHUB_USERNAME/_TOKEN secrets), BUILD_VERSION build-arg = tag on a tag-push else 'edge', gha cache. Per project memory the GHCR image is PRIVATE by default. Dockerfile builds CONTROLLER ONLY (agent is host-installed), static CGO-free, runs as non-root uid 65532, bare-binary ENTRYPOINT so `create-operator` subcommand works, /api/health HEALTHCHECK.

DEFECTS / RISKS in the release pipeline:
1. THE RELEASE GATE IS A STRICT SUBSET OF CI, NOT A MIRROR (despite the comment 'Mirrors ci.yml's two jobs'). gate-go OMITS the gofmt-drift check, and gate-frontend runs lint ONLY (the tsc -b build is deferred to build-frontend). So a tag pointing at a commit with gofmt drift, or one that fails tsc -b but passes lint, would FAIL only deep in build-frontend (or pass with drift) rather than at the gate. Since tags can point anywhere, the gate should be byte-identical to ci.yml's gates. Add gofmt-drift to gate-go and move/duplicate the tsc -b build into gate-frontend (or just run `npm run build` there).
2. Dockerfile pins golang:1.25-alpine while release.yml/ci.yml use go-version-file: go.mod (go 1.25.0). Two different toolchain-resolution mechanisms for the SAME release; no explicit `toolchain` directive in go.mod to bind them. A go.mod bump to 1.26 would silently leave the Docker image on 1.25 until someone hand-edits the Dockerfile. Low probability, but it means the containerized controller and the matrix binaries are not guaranteed built with the same toolchain.
3. release.yml does NOT run -race either (acceptable for a build pipeline, but combined with CI also lacking it, -race never runs on a tagged release).
4. release.yml and ci.yml duplicate gate logic by hand (two copies that can drift) — the root cause of defect #1. A reusable workflow (workflow_call) would single-source the gate.
recommendations: HIGHEST VALUE — Add `-race` to the Go test step in ci.yml (and ideally release.yml gate-go): `go test -race ./...`. The suite already passes -race locally (~14s for the heavy trio) and the load-bearing regression scenario #9 (GetServedConfig atomicity under concurrent promote) plus the FileStore global-mutex / tenantlock paths were written specifically to be raced. Without it, a concurrency regression in the served/staged split would ship undetected. Low cost (the full suite is fast), high payoff.

HIGHEST VALUE — Stand up a frontend test runner (vitest) and cover the four pure Go-mirror helpers FIRST: lib/normalizeEdges.ts healCollidingPins, lib/updateStatus.ts deriveUpdateState, lib/custody.ts stripPrivateKeys/dropAllKeys, lib/webauthn.ts base64url/byte encoding. These are the silent cross-boundary break points — any Go model/tag/marker change breaks them with zero current test gate, and custody/dropAllKeys is a zero-knowledge SECURITY invariant. They are already authored as pure functions explicitly so a runner could cover them. Add a `test` script to frontend/package.json and a `test` step to ci.yml's frontend job. This is the single biggest coverage hole and the most direct path to rc.1 confidence on the FE.

Make the release gate a TRUE mirror of CI (fix the strict-subset defect): add the gofmt-drift check to release.yml gate-go, and run `npm run build` (tsc -b) in gate-frontend, not just lint. Best done by extracting ci.yml's two gate jobs into a reusable workflow (workflow_call) that both ci.yml and release.yml invoke, so the gate is single-sourced and cannot drift. A tag must not be able to ship code that would have failed PR CI.

Pin one toolchain source for releases: either bump the Dockerfile FROM to match go.mod via a build-arg, or (cleaner) add an explicit `toolchain go1.25.x` directive to go.mod and reference it consistently, so the GHCR controller image and the matrix binaries are provably built with the same Go version. Prevents silent skew on the next Go bump.

Add the FIRST cmd/* tests, targeting the brick-risk and bootstrap paths over the daemon loop: (a) a cmd/server test that boots NewServer in air-gap and controller mode and asserts the two-port/gateAirgap wiring + graceful SIGTERM drain; (b) a cmd/server/operator.go create-operator round-trip into a temp FileStore; (c) a cmd/agent test (or promote the existing internal/agent reconcile coverage to the main package) pinning the PhaseA->PhaseB->Finalize call-ORDER, since that ordering is a documented load-bearing invariant spread across two packages and the binary itself is currently 0% covered. Highest-value of the three is (c).

Add a coverage-regression guard to CI (e.g. `go test -coverprofile` + a threshold check, or a third-party action), even a loose one (fail if any package drops >10 points), so the strong existing coverage (most internal packages 82-100%) cannot silently erode. Pair with documenting that the agent's 69.8% headline is dominated by untestable host I/O glue (renameOrCopy/HTTPSource), and that the security DECISION logic is at 100% — so the floor should be set per-package, not a single global number.

Cover the host-side self-update binary-swap mechanics: add a unit test for renameOrCopy (7.7%) and copyFile (50%) exercising the cross-filesystem EXDEV fallback path with a temp dir on the same fs and a simulated rename failure. This is the literal moment a partial write could brick an agent; the decision to swap is fully tested but the swap mechanics are not.

Optional hardening: add a `-count=1` to one CI run (or a nightly job) to defeat the build cache and surface any not-yet-observed flakiness, and add a lightweight CI doc-link check to catch the existing broken docs/spec -> archived implementation_plans links and future drift. Both are low-priority relative to -race and the FE runner.

gaps.missing: The investigation's compile-DoS/robustness work focused on CPU/memory blowup in the allocator and peer-derivation, but did NOT check that panic recovery actually covers the controller-mode surfaces. VERIFIED GAP: only the 4 air-gap routes + /health (server.go:116-120, registerRoutes via wrap/compute) are wrapped in s.recoverPanics. RegisterOperatorRoutes (handler_controller.go:213, op = cors(operatorAuth(...))) and RegisterAgentRoutes (handler_controller.go:195, bare h.requireNode/HandleEnroll/HandleBootstrap) have NO recoverPanics wrapper, and ListenAndServeAgent mounts s.agentMux directly (server.go:237). The recoverPanics doc comment (server.go:158-160) explicitly cites allocator panics being turned into clean 5xx — but that protection does not extend to the operator or agent handlers, which fall back to net/http's per-request recover (logs + drops the connection, no apierr JSON envelope, no CodeInternalPanic). A nil-deref or index panic in HandleConfig/HandleEnroll/HandleStage etc. yields a torn connection rather than a coded error.
gaps.why_it_matters: Controller mode is the login-gated, fleet-bearing mode that rc.1 is gating on. A panic in any operator/agent handler is a robustness/availability hole the charter's compile-DoS coverage implies is closed (the allocator panic-to-500 claim) but is only closed on the air-gap mux. Cheap to fix (wrap both muxes); important to verify before rc.1.

gaps.missing: writeJSONAtomic (filestore.go:215-231) does os.WriteFile(tmp) then os.Rename(tmp,path) with NO fsync of the temp file and NO fsync of the parent directory. On power loss / hard crash the rename can be persisted ahead of the data blocks (or the dirent not durable), leaving a zero-length or truncated 'committed' record despite the temp-rename pattern. The audit JSONL append (filestore.go:1925) is a bare O_APPEND write, also unsynced. The code comments claim 'a crash can never leave a half-written record' (filestore.go:21-23) — true for torn writes, but NOT for reorder-on-rename without fsync. No test exercises crash/torn-write recovery; store_compat_test/settings_test/etc. are happy-path. The FileStore SPOF (global mutex + 200ms generation poll) is explicitly deferred to rc.2/GA in STATUS.md, but the fsync durability gap is distinct and not captured.
gaps.why_it_matters: The FileStore is the controller's source of truth for node identities, bearer-token hashes, the operator credential, the signing anchor, and the served trust-list. A non-durable write of any of these on an unclean shutdown can silently corrupt fleet trust state (e.g. a half-written nodes/<id>.json or trust-list). For a server-authoritative release this is a data-integrity risk worth at least documenting as a known limitation alongside the already-acknowledged SPOF.

gaps.missing: The passkey LOGIN path (handler_passkey.go:177 -> trustlist.VerifyAssertion, webauthn.go:51) was mapped under crypto but two security-relevant design choices were not surfaced as findings: (1) the clientDataJSON.origin check is conditional on pin.Origin being non-empty (webauthn.go:170) — if an operator credential is pinned WITHOUT an Origin, there is NO relying-party origin binding at all, only the rpIdHash check (sha256(pin.RPID)). The comment even calls origin 'advisory' on a node, but for the LOGIN (panel) path origin is the primary anti-phishing binding and an empty-Origin pin silently disables it. (2) The signature counter is intentionally never checked (webauthn.go:19-20,163-167) to support synced passkeys — so cloned-authenticator detection is off by design. Neither is necessarily wrong, but neither was assessed against the controller-mode auth boundary the review covers.
gaps.why_it_matters: Passkey login is one of the three operator auth factors and the one with the strongest claim (phishing-resistant). Whether origin binding is actually enforced depends on operator pin configuration; a release should either require a non-empty Origin for login pins or document that origin binding is config-gated. The counter decision is defensible but should be an explicit, recorded acceptance.

gaps.area: compile-DoS reachability / severity context for the 3 DoS findings
gaps.missing: The three compile-DoS findings (allocator-cidr-reserved-cpu-blowup HIGH, unbounded-domains-and-reserved-ranges, peers-gapfill-quadratic) are characterized as unauthenticated, which is correct for LOCAL/air-gap mode (server.go:116-120 — /compile,/validate,/export are gateAirgap-passthrough when operatorAuth is nil). What is NOT spelled out: in CONTROLLER mode those same routes are operator-auth-gated (EnableController arms s.operatorAuth, gateAirgap wraps them), AND the node-facing /config path serves a PRE-STAGED bundle — node-triggered deploys do not run compiler.Compile (CompileAndStage is operator-only, compile.go). So the DoS trigger surface is: anonymous in local mode, authenticated-operator in controller mode, never a lower-trust node. This materially scopes the HIGH finding's blast radius and should be recorded so the fix priority is set correctly (it is a self-DoS / availability issue for a local design tool, not a fleet-node-reachable break).
gaps.why_it_matters: Severity and fix urgency for the headline HIGH DoS finding hinge on who can reach it. Without the mode-reachability scoping, the finding could be over- or under-prioritized for rc.1.

gaps.area: export/ZIP in-memory amplification (a 4th DoS vector not enumerated)
gaps.missing: createExportZip (handler.go:353-355) and tarGzDirectory (handler.go:410-412) build the ENTIRE archive into a bytes.Buffer in memory before writing the response. On the unauthenticated /api/export route (local mode) a max-size topology (2000 nodes) renders per-node bundles (configs, scripts, keys) all buffered in RAM simultaneously, plus the compile cost. This is a memory-amplification axis distinct from the CPU findings (allocator/peers) — the request body cap (maxRequestBodyBytes, MaxBytesReader at handler.go:330) bounds INPUT but not the multiplicatively larger rendered OUTPUT held in memory. No measurement of peak RSS for a 2000-node export was taken.
gaps.why_it_matters: Completes the compile-DoS surface: the investigation measured CPU blowup but not the memory blowup of the export/render output path, which is reachable on the same unauthenticated route and could OOM the process (which recoverPanics does NOT catch, per the panic-recovery gap above).

gaps.missing: ~14k LOC of TypeScript (frontend/src) with ZERO test runner (confirmed: no *.test/*.spec files; CI runs only npm run lint + npm run build; STATUS.md lists 'a frontend test runner' as deferred). The investigation tagged frontend as 'structure-drift + phone' and examined custody.ts, but did NOT security-review the auth-bearing client paths: (a) where/whether the operator session token ever lands in localStorage vs httpOnly cookie (the whole appshell-redesign claim is 'no token in JS' — owed smoke #1 still open); (b) the double-submit CSRF token handling client-side (api/ layer) matching auth_controller.go:162; (c) XSS/output-encoding when rendering compiled configs, node names, audit-log entries, and error envelopes (error.<code> localization) into the DOM — node-supplied/operator-supplied strings flow into the panel; (d) whether dropAllKeys/stripPrivateKeys are actually invoked on EVERY controller-mode write path (custody.ts is correct in isolation, but call-site coverage was not traced).
gaps.why_it_matters: Controller mode's trust boundary is half client-side (custody key-stripping, token storage, CSRF). A backend-only review leaves the panel's auth and key-custody enforcement, and any DOM-injection vector from fleet/audit data, unverified — and there is no automated test to catch regressions. This is the single largest unreviewed code mass relative to its security weight.

gaps.missing: CI (ci.yml) runs only go vet + go test (no -race) + npm lint + npm build. MISSING for an rc: (1) go test -race (the FileStore mutex, the 200ms WaitForGeneration poller, concurrent enroll/poll/stage, and the login rate-limiter loginratelimit.go are all concurrency-bearing and untested under the race detector); (2) govulncheck (Go stdlib + wgctrl/x-crypto CVE scanning — none); (3) gosec/semgrep static analysis; (4) any frontend test execution. release.yml mirrors ci.yml's gates (no race/vuln scan) and builds CGO_ENABLED=0.
gaps.why_it_matters: For a security-sensitive rc.1, the absence of a race gate is notable given the per-username login-lockout finding and the global-mutex store; a data race in token/identity bookkeeping would be a correctness+security bug that the current gates cannot catch. govulncheck is a cheap, high-value addition before a release tag.

gaps.area: unauth-agent finding cluster: completeness of the takeover model
gaps.missing: The three confirmed unauth-agent findings (enroll-overwrites-live-node-identity, revoke-does-not-invalidate-enrollment-tokens, enroll-token-ttl-unbounded) are characterized but the investigation did not verify two adjacent properties that determine the real blast radius: (a) whether HandleBootstrap (the OTHER unauth agent route, handler_controller.go:204) can be abused similarly or leaks the served install script / settings to an anonymous caller (it is served with NO auth by design); (b) whether the agent /report path (requireNode) trusts any node-supplied field that influences other nodes' state or the operator's view (e.g. a malicious node reporting a forged applied-generation or version to corrupt the rollout/canary state machine or the per-node update chip). The custody invariant ('a node can only act as itself', context-not-body) was verified for identity, but report-payload trust was not.
gaps.why_it_matters: The enroll-takeover findings establish that an enrollment token is a node-takeover capability; the natural completeness question is whether a node, once enrolled (or a compromised node), can influence MORE than its own record via /report or whether /bootstrap leaks provisioning data to anonymous callers. Both are within the unauth/low-trust-agent surface the review owns.

surfaces_not_covered: Frontend security review: operator-token storage (localStorage vs httpOnly), client-side CSRF double-submit, DOM/XSS encoding of fleet/audit/config strings, custody call-site coverage — ~14k LOC TS with no test runner and only 1 of 5 owed manual smokes (browser login) still open

Concurrency / race-detector coverage: FileStore global mutex, WaitForGeneration 200ms poller, concurrent enroll/poll/stage/rekey, login rate-limiter — never run under go test -race; CI has no race gate

Crash/durability testing of FileStore: no fsync (data or parent dir) on writeJSONAtomic; bare O_APPEND audit log; no torn-write/power-loss recovery test for node identities, operator credential, signing anchor, served trust-list
Panic-recovery on controller (s.mux operator routes) and agent (s.agentMux) muxes — only the 4 air-gap routes are recoverPanics-wrapped
Memory-amplification DoS on /api/export (whole ZIP/tar.gz buffered in RAM for a 2000-node topology) — distinct from the measured CPU DoS
Dependency vulnerability scanning (govulncheck) and SAST (gosec) — absent from CI and release pipeline
Agent /report and /bootstrap trust model: whether a node can corrupt cross-node/rollout state via forged report fields, and whether unauth /bootstrap leaks provisioning/settings data
WebAuthn login origin-binding enforcement when pin.Origin is empty (relying-party origin check silently disabled) and the deliberate signature-counter opt-out (clone detection off)

MemStore vs FileStore parity: MemStore (memstore.go, ~32k) is a second Store implementation — whether its security-relevant invariants (token uniqueness, revoke, generation monotonicity) match FileStore was not confirmed (used in tests; if ever reachable in a deployment mode it is a parallel surface)
recommended_followups: Wrap RegisterOperatorRoutes and RegisterAgentRoutes (or the agentMux/operator-mux at the http.Server.Handler level) in recoverPanics so a panic in any controller/agent handler returns a coded 500 JSON envelope instead of a torn connection; add a test that panics a controller handler and asserts CodeInternalPanic.
Add go test -race and govulncheck to ci.yml and release.yml before cutting rc.1; consider gosec. Run the suite once under -race to surface any token/store data race.

Run a dedicated frontend security pass on the auth-bearing TS: confirm no operator token in localStorage (close owed smoke #1 in code, not just browser), trace CSRF double-submit against auth_controller.go:162, audit DOM encoding of node-supplied / audit-log / compiled-config strings, and verify dropAllKeys/stripPrivateKeys fire on every controller-mode write call-site.

Add fsync of the temp file AND the parent directory to writeJSONAtomic (and fsync the audit append), or explicitly document the durability limitation in STATUS.md alongside the already-noted FileStore SPOF.

Scope the three compile-DoS findings by reachability (anonymous in local mode; operator-only in controller mode; never node-reachable) and record it on each finding so fix priority is set correctly; measure peak RSS of a 2000-node /api/export and decide whether to stream the archive instead of buffering.

Decide and record a policy on WebAuthn login pins: require a non-empty Origin for the login (panel) credential so origin binding is always enforced, and explicitly accept (in writing) the signature-counter opt-out for synced passkeys.
Verify the agent /report payload trust model (can a node forge applied-generation/version to corrupt rollout/canary or another node's chip?) and what unauth /bootstrap discloses to anonymous callers.

Confirm MemStore enforces the same security invariants as FileStore (token uniqueness on UpsertNode, revoke clears the reverse index, generation monotonicity) or document that MemStore is test-only and never a deployment Store.

key_files.responsibility: Server struct + the two-mux wiring (s.mux operator/air-gap port, s.agentMux agent port). EnableController arms operator auth; gateAirgap (read at request time) makes /api/validate|compile|export|deploy-script operator-gated in controller mode and passthrough in local mode. Holds recoverPanics, the permissive air-gap cors, Slowloris-timeout *http.Server construction (ListenAndServe / ListenAndServeAgent), and context-cancel-first graceful Shutdown.

Air-gap (unauthenticated in local mode) compute handlers: HandleHealth/Validate/Compile/Export/DeployScript. Owns readTopology (4MiB MaxBytesReader cap), the wire error envelope structs (apiError/errorBody) + the single error-write path writeJSON/writeAPIError/writeCodedOr, and the self-extracting installer ZIP builder (createExportZip/tarGzDirectory/makeSelfExtractingInstaller with optional Ed25519 sig + SHA-256 integrity block).

GOD-FILE. ControllerHandler struct + both route tables (RegisterAgentRoutes ~195, RegisterOperatorRoutes ~213), the credentialed/non-credentialed operator cors (~320), path-prefix setters (Operator/AgentBasePath), ~30 JSON wire types, and most controller handlers: enroll/config/poll/report/rekey (agent) + update-topology/stage/compile-preview/promote/nodes/revoke/clear-rekey/audit/topology(/versions)/enrollment-token/rekey-all + keystone trio operator-credential/trustlist/trustlist-signature. Plus shared helpers identity/decodeJSON/readControllerBody/parseAfter.

The auth chokepoint. bearerToken parser; authenticateNode (hash-keyed Store.LookupNodeByAPIToken -> 401); requireNode (injects tenant+node from CONTEXT, never body); operatorAuth (Bearer session/break-glass OR httpOnly cookie + double-submit CSRF on state-changing methods); resolveOperator (session lookup, then subtle.ConstantTimeCompare break-glass, fail-closed). Defines the ctxKey type + tenantFromCtx/nodeFromCtx accessors.

HandleLogin orchestration: atomic rate-limit reserve-before-password-work, argon2 VerifyPassword (+ DummyVerifyPassword for unknown-user timing parity), second-factor gate (PASSKEY precedence over TOTP, atomic AdvanceTOTPStep to burn the step), mintSessionResponse (shared success tail for all login paths), auditLockout, HandleLogout.

httpOnly session/CSRF cookie machinery: setSessionCookies/clearSessionCookies (SameSite from secureCookie), sessionCookieToken/csrfValid (double-submit), isStateChanging, newCSRFToken (CSPRNG), HandleSession probe (panel derives login state post-refresh), bearerOrCookieToken.

WebAuthn passkey: login begin/finish (passwordless) + register/disable/status (operator-managed factor). issueLoginChallenge (single-use, 5min TTL), writePasskeyChallenge, verifyLoginAssertion against the keystone trust-list pin; pinFromLoginCredential bridges controller.LoginCredential -> trustlist.PinnedCredential.

Operator GET/POST /settings (read/update ControllerSettings: agent URL, gh-proxy, release URL) with URL/mimic-catalog/rollout validators, and the UNAUTH agent GET /bootstrap that renders an embedded bash install+enroll+apply script (bakes the pinned off-host operator credential when keystone is on). shQuote for safe interpolation.

Operator-only assisted release-pin fetch (HandleReleasePins). SSRF egress guard: newReleasePinClient with a Dialer.Control hook (blockPrivateAddr/isPublicUnicastIP, post-DNS, defeats rebinding + NAT64-embedded private v4), redirect cap, body cap. Convenience-only: explicitly NOT a trust anchor (trust = keystone-signed artifacts.json).
TOTP second-factor management for the current operator: status/enroll/confirm/disable. currentOperator helper resolves the session identity from context for the management routes.

In-memory failed-attempt limiter (loginLimiter): registerAttempt is an atomic check-and-reserve keyed by user + source IP; succeed refunds; pruneLocked GCs; clientIP extracts the source. Two instances in ControllerHandler: loginLimiter (login) and a separate higher-cap enrollLimiter (enroll spray).

Opt-in SPA file server (EnableStatic, gated on YAOG_WEB_DIR) mounted at / on s.mux; spaHandler serves real files (traversal-safe via http.Dir) and falls back to index.html for client routes, but 404s any /api/, /api/v1/operator/, /api/v1/agent/ path so routing typos are not masked.

Stdlib-only LEAF error-code layer (NOT under internal/api). Defines Code constants (~100+), Error type with Status()/Message()/Params()/With()/Wrap(), and the code->HTTP-status + English-template registry. The single source of HTTP statuses; consumed by the whole api region via writeAPIError/writeCodedOr and returned 'coded at the source' by deep validator/compiler/render packages.

dependencies: DOWNWARD (api imports these internal pkgs): apierr (leaf error codes/status — pervasive), controller (Store, identities, HashToken, VerifyPassword/VerifyTOTP, sessions, settings, audit, OperatorCredential — the controller-mode backbone), model (Topology wire type), compiler (NewCompiler/Compile — air-gap path), render (GenerateKeys, All, FetchSettingsFromEnv — air-gap path), renderer (RenderAllBabelConfigs/RenderDeployScripts/Artifact — air-gap + release-pins), validator (ValidateSchema/ValidateSemantic), artifacts (Export), bundlesig (LoadConfigSignerFromEnv/ConfigSigner — installer signing), naming (SafeInstallerFileName), normalize (HealCollidingPins, via controller compile path), trustlist (TrustList/Verify/PinnedCredential — keystone signature flow).

INWARD (who imports internal/api): ONLY cmd/server/main.go (NewServer, EnableController, EnableStatic, NewControllerHandler + setters, ListenAndServe[Agent]). No other internal package imports api — it sits at the top of the graph.

EXTERNAL libs: NONE in non-test files. The entire region is Go stdlib (net/http, crypto/subtle, crypto/sha256, archive/*, compress/gzip, encoding/*) + internal packages only. Third-party (wgctrl/x-crypto) is reached transitively through render/controller, never directly.
CYCLE CHECK: clean. apierr is a documented stdlib-only leaf (imports nothing internal); controller does NOT import api; api is a strict sink. No backwards or cyclic deps found.
public_surface: Server lifecycle (server.go): NewServer() *Server; (*Server).EnableController(*ControllerHandler); (*Server).EnableStatic(dir); (*Server).ListenAndServe(addr); (*Server).ListenAndServeAgent(addr); (*Server).Shutdown(ctx); (*Server).Handler()/AgentHandler() (test seams).

ControllerHandler ctor + config (handler_controller.go): NewControllerHandler(store, tenant, operatorTokenHash, operatorName); DefaultOperatorName const; setters SetPanelOrigins / SetSecureCookie / SetOperatorPathPrefix / SetAgentPathPrefix; OperatorBasePath() / AgentBasePath(); RegisterOperatorRoutes(mux) / RegisterAgentRoutes(mux).
Handler ctor (handler.go): NewHandler() *Handler; the HandleHealth/Validate/Compile/Export/DeployScript methods (registered by server.go).
AGENT ROUTES (agent port, under /api/v1/agent/): POST /enroll (unauth), GET /config, GET /poll?after=N, POST /report, POST /rekey (all requireNode bearer), GET /bootstrap (unauth).

OPERATOR ROUTES (operator/panel port, under /api/v1/operator/): UNAUTH /login, /login/passkey/begin, /login/passkey/finish; operatorAuth-gated /logout /session /totp/* /passkey/* /update-topology /stage /compile-preview /promote /nodes /revoke /audit /topology /topology/versions /enrollment-token /rekey-all /clear-rekey /settings /release-pins /operator-credential /trustlist /trustlist-signature.

AIR-GAP ROUTES (operator/panel port, under /api/): GET /api/health (always public), POST /api/validate /api/compile /api/export /api/deploy-script (operator-gated in controller mode via gateAirgap, open in local mode).

Cross-region contracts other regions rely on: the nested error envelope {error:{code,message,params}} (writeAPIError) and apierr.Code as the FE localization join key; the auth context contract (handlers read node identity from context via nodeFromCtx/identity, never from the body).
smells: GOD-FILE: handler_controller.go is 1964 lines — 2.4x the >800 threshold and >3x the next-largest non-test file. It mixes the agent surface, the operator surface, CORS, ~30 wire types, the keystone signing flow, and shared helpers. The login/totp/passkey/bootstrap/cookie/release_pins handlers were split out into sibling files but the core controller dispatch was not; this file is the region's primary structural risk.

PACKAGE-NAME COLLISION (region-adjacent): internal/render vs internal/renderer are two different packages both imported by handler.go (lines 26-27) and the region uses both — render.GenerateKeys/All vs renderer.RenderAllBabelConfigs/RenderDeployScripts. Easy to confuse; flagged in the brief as a structure smell to assess. The naming gives no hint which is the entry point (render is) vs the low-level templates (renderer).

DUPLICATED cors: there are TWO independent cors implementations — Server.cors in server.go (permissive '*', air-gap) and ControllerHandler.cors in handler_controller.go (~320, credentialed-allowlist + Vary). Justified (different trust models) but a reader must not assume one covers the other; the air-gap routes get the permissive one even when operator-gated.

TRUNCATED/STUB comments (the CJK/English smell noted in the brief): server.go:16 '// Server HTTP API', :52 '// NewServer  API', :123 '// cors CORS'; handler.go:31 '// Handler HTTP API', :36 '// NewHandler  API'; plus mixed-language inline comments (server.go:102-104, 139-160 all Chinese; handler.go has Chinese block comments at 79-83, 263-266, 312, 473-477). Doc quality is uneven — some functions have excellent English doc-comments, others are one-word stubs.

WIRE-TYPE SPRAWL: ~30 *JSON request/response structs live inline in handler_controller.go (347-585) rather than a types file, inflating the god-file and making the wire contract hard to survey in one place.

DUPLICATED body-reader pattern: handler.go:readTopology and handler_controller.go:readControllerBody/decodeJSON each independently set http.MaxBytesReader + errors.As(MaxBytesError); two near-identical size-cap idioms with two different limit constants (maxRequestBodyBytes 4MiB vs controllerMaxBodyBytes).

gateAirgap indirection: the air-gap routes' operator-auth gating is wired via a request-time-read struct field (s.operatorAuth) set by EnableController AFTER registerRoutes, rather than at registration — a deliberate but subtle ordering dependency that a reader can miss (documented at server.go:29-36,87-99).
region_maps.notes: "For downstream investigators: (1) Two ports/two muxes is the central mental model — agent surface (per-node bearer, requireNode) is fully separate from operator surface (session/cookie+CSRF/break-glass, operatorAuth) and from the air-gap compute surface (gated only in controller mode). cmd/server/main.go is the ONLY caller; read main.go:104-205 to see local-vs-controller wiring (NewServer always; EnableController only under the controller env gate). (2) HTTP status codes are NOT chosen in handlers — they come from apierr's code->status registry; to change a status, edit internal/apierr/apierr.go, not the handler. Handlers almost always go through writeAPIError(apierr.New(Code)) or writeCodedOr(fallback, err). (3) The 'node acts only as itself' invariant is enforced in auth_controller.go:requireNode (identity from context, not body) and relied on by every agent handler via identity()/nodeFromCtx — do not add a handler that trusts a node_id from the request body. (4) operatorAuth dual-path (Bearer exempt from CSRF; cookie path requires double-submit CSRF on state-changing methods) is the subtlest auth logic — see auth_controller.go:150-180 + cookie_session.go. (5) Keystone trust-list signing (handler_controller.go:1771-1897) has a strict substitution guard: the operator must sign EXACTLY the re-derived staged-manifest canonical bytes (bytes.Equal at 1849), verified against the PINNED credential before persist. (6) release_pins.go is the only egress-making code in the region and is explicitly a convenience, NOT a trust anchor — its SSRF guard (blockPrivateAddr) is structurally important and intentionally rejects loopback, so its happy path is only testable via an injected releaseClient field. (7) Test coverage is heavy: ~24 test files including a 1193-line controller_http_test.go and dedicated keystone_repro/keystone_rotation/airgap_auth_gate suites — the test:source line ratio is ~roughly 1:1, so behavior is well-pinned."

The one-way import is documented but the boundary ('what signs the bundle' vs 'what signs the membership') is non-obvious; trustlist.SignedTrustList vs bundlesig signatures vs SigningAnchor (3 distinct signature/anchor concepts) is a lot of crypto surface to keep straight.

AUDIT CHAIN is tamper-EVIDENT ONLY (self-documented): VerifyAuditChain anchors on the first retained entry's PrevHash, so post-rotation prefix-truncation is NOT detectable and any actor with store write access can re-forge forward. Honest, but a reviewer must not mistake it for a cryptographic guarantee (real anti-tamper deferred to 'Plan 5').

region_maps.notes:
CRYPTO TRUST MODEL (downstream investigators): two independent signature chains. (1) BUNDLE signing — bundlesig signs the canonical checksums.sha256 (covers install.sh + all configs) with YAOG_BUNDLE_SIGNING_KEY (Ed25519, TOFU-pinned via SigningAnchor; enforceSigningAnchor in compile.go fails LOUD on drop/swap with apierr CodeSigningKeyMissing/Mismatch). (2) KEYSTONE membership — the OFF-HOST operator credential signs the trustlist.TrustList manifest binding each node's bundleSHA256=hex(sha256(checksums.sha256)). trustlist.Verify dispatches on the PINNED alg (algorithm-confusion guard) and is fail-closed.

SERVED-vs-STAGED SPLIT is the central recently-fixed invariant (beta.5): PutSignedTrustList/GetCurrentSignedTrustList = STAGED slot (to-be-signed, mid-deploy); GetServedTrustList = SERVED slot (last-promoted, what /config hands the fleet). PromoteStaged copies staged->served only AFTER the off-host signature verifies. GetServedConfig reads (bundle, keystone-on, served-manifest) under ONE store lock so a concurrent promote can't expose a torn (old-bundle,new-manifest) pair. Cross-PROCESS crash mid-promote is fail-closed + self-repairing (agent rejects digest mismatch, re-run rewrites served slot) — documented as intentional, NOT a generation-tagged atomic write.

PROMOTE GATE IS DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH, NOT THE TRUST ROOT (compile.go:582 explicit): the controller's PromoteStaged verifies the off-host signature over the staged manifest but does NOT re-derive bundle digests; the AUTHORITATIVE chokepoint is the AGENT re-deriving hex(sha256(checksums.sha256)) offline (internal/agent/verify.go). Don't mistake the controller gate for the root.

MONOTONIC EPOCH (anti-rollback): stageManifest chains epoch off the STAGED slot (not served) — reuse if membership tuple {wg_key,bundle_sha256} identical, else +1. Because BundleSHA256 is in the membership tuple, ANY install.sh/config change advances the epoch. Comment warns NOT to 'simplify' this to read the served slot (would lose monotonicity across un-promoted re-stages).

CONCURRENCY: tenantlock.go serializes the multi-Store-call CompileAndStage/PromoteStaged per tenant IN-PROCESS (sync.Map of mutexes) on top of each store's own per-op lock; matches the single-process FileStore contract. FileStore has a documented SPOF: ONE global mutex for all ops + 200ms-poll WaitForGeneration (no condvar). Deferred to rc.2/GA, not a correctness bug.

CUSTODY: enrolledSubgraph stamps WGPublicKey from the registry and CLEARS WireGuardPrivateKey; CompileSubgraph runs render.GenerateKeys in AgentHeld mode (placeholder private keys) — zero-knowledge end to end. The render.GenerateKeys wgtypes dependency (noted in the brief) is the WASM/browser-portability blocker and lives OUTSIDE this region (internal/render).

WEBAUTHN reuse: trustlist/webauthn.go's verifyAssertion core serves BOTH the content-bound keystone path (challenge=SHA256(Canonical(tl))) AND the random-nonce operator passkey LOGIN path (VerifyAssertion + AssertionChallenge + Store.ConsumeLoginChallenge). Core Run() control loop: pull->verify->membership-keystone->anti-rollback->(forced self-update)->stage->apply(install.sh)->report->(deferred self-update). Plus stage/apply/recordSuccess/recordFailure with monotonic-floor preservation of MembershipEpoch + self-update custody state.

Fail-closed integrity gates BEFORE install.sh: VerifyBundle (Ed25519 sig over canonical checksums.sha256 + per-file SHA-256 + install.sh/artifacts.json coverage guards) and VerifyMembership (off-host keystone trust-list: canonical-bytes contract, pinned-alg dispatch, bundle-digest binding, membership + epoch anti-rollback). Also credFingerprint helpers and WG-conf peer-key extraction.

Signed agent self-update (the risky core): parseAgentCatalog, pure decideSelfUpdate/isForced, performSelfUpdate (download->verify-against-signed-pin->self-test->breadcrumb->install-then-flip->re-exec), and the boot reconcile state machine ReconcileSelfUpdateEarly/Promote + FinalizeSelfUpdate + rollbackAndAbandon.

ControllerClient: plain-HTTP per-node bearer client for /enroll(unauth)+/config+/poll(long-poll)+/rekey+/report. Implements agent.Source (Fetch) and agent.Reporter (Report). Mirrors server wire JSON as private structs; tracks lastFetchedGen/lastRekeyRequested/priorGen for watermark + failed-apply reporting.

Package doc + Source/Reporter interfaces; DirSource (air-gap, walks Root/<nodeID>/) and HTTPSource (checksums-first fetch + tolerant 404 for optional control files) implementations; NewSourceFromSpec parses dir:/http(s):// specs.

Persisted State struct (last-applied + MembershipEpoch + self-update floor/PendingUpdate/AbandonedAgentVersion), atomic Load/SaveState, manifest.json parsing, and CheckRollback (compiled_at anti-rollback, corrupt-baseline tolerant).

RunControllerCycle: the testable one-cycle controller-pull unit (Poll->Fetch->REKEY|IDLE|APPLY branches) with the BLOCKER-2 watermark-advance fix and the plan-3 idle-skip; keep-last-good on every error.

compareVersions: a documented, dependency-free SemVer-ish comparator (release core + pre-release precedence; empty-string is the minimal sentinel) backing all self-update floor/downgrade/min-version decisions.

WireGuard private-key custody: EnsureKey (idempotent, never rotates identity), RegenerateKey (explicit rekey rotation), shared atomic generateAndWriteKey (0600, temp+rename). The only wgtypes user in the region.
ReprovisionKeystone: atomic, validate-before-write rewrite of the pinned off-host operator credential PEM (out-of-band rotated keystone adoption); fail-closed on empty/unparsable PEM; no network I/O.

region_maps.notes: "WASM/browser-portability: keygen.go is the only wgtypes user here (keygen.go:9), mirroring the render.go keygen blocker the brief flags; the rest of the package is stdlib + bundlesig/trustlist and would port. Self-update uses syscall.Exec (selfupdate.go:42, indirected via execFn for tests) and os/exec — inherently host-only, never browser-side. AIR-GAP vs CONTROLLER split: agent.Run is mode-agnostic; SelfUpdate/keystone are gated by nil-ness of cfg.SelfUpdate / cfg.OperatorCredPEM, so DirSource (air-gap, cmd/compiler) deploys never self-update and behave exactly as pre-plan-9. SECURITY-CRITICAL invariants downstream investigators must preserve: (1) VerifyBundle + VerifyMembership run BEFORE any root-side install.sh (agent.go:118-141); (2) the bundle-digest binding in VerifyMembership (verify.go:384-399) is THE check that makes the off-host signature cover what RUNS, not just the membership list — a breached controller cannot re-sign checksums.sha256; (3) self-update verifies downloaded bytes against the SIGNED artifacts.json pin, never the upstream .sha256 sidecar (selfupdate.go:204-212), and the artifacts.json coverage guard (verify.go:225-229) stops an unlisted artifacts.json being smuggled in; (4) MembershipEpoch and AgentVersionFloor are MONOTONIC and preserved across recordSuccess/recordFailure (agent.go:328-340, 362-371) — a keystone-OFF run must not reset the floor to 0; (5) the 'swapped bool' contract: once performSelfUpdate has swapped the on-disk binary, a re-exec failure must NOT recordFailure (would erase the crash-durable breadcrumb -> unbounded crash loop, agent.go:174-188). KNOWN/DOCUMENTED LIMITS to carry forward: anti-rollback on compiled_at only guards an honest stale source, NOT an active MITM (compiled_at is left out of the signed set — agent.go:143-148, attacker-resistant version is Phase 2/3); reprovision-keystone is same-alg only (a different alg/rpid/origin needs a fresh bootstrap/unit edit — main.go:186); self-update is certified amd64/arm64 only and fails closed otherwise (selfupdate.go:171). The crash-loop bound (maxSelfUpdateAttempts=3) lives entirely in the boot reconcile (no systemd unit change). Recent git history (f3fb339, 28d3fd5) shows the served-vs-staged trust-list split + keystone regression suite just landed; keystone_test.go/keystone_regression_test.go are the load-bearing adversarial coverage for verify.go."

region_maps.notes:
Build/typecheck: `npm run build` = `tsc -b && vite build` (project references: tsconfig.app.json for src, tsconfig.node.json for config). Per MEMORY, ALWAYS verify FE with `npm run build` not bare tsc --noEmit (tsc -b is stricter; CI caught a TS2352 the loose root config missed). tsconfig.app.json is strict + noUnusedLocals/Params + verbatimModuleSyntax + erasableSyntaxOnly. dist/ is gitignored build output (not tracked). 

TWO OPERATING MODES are the central organizing axis of this region, gated by useControllerStore.mode ('local'|'controller', persisted): LOCAL = topologyStore is authoritative + persisted to localStorage; talks to UNAUTH air-gap routes; compile/export refused-with-guard in controller mode. CONTROLLER = server-authoritative; canvas is a non-persisted secret mirror (canvasFromServer invariant: when true+controller, partialize blanks project/domains/nodes/edges so fleet public-IPs/SSH never hit localStorage, and logout/session-loss/switchToLocal flush it). 

SECURITY-CRITICAL invariants concentrated in the two stores (downstream reviewers should focus here): (1) zero-knowledge custody — stripPrivateKeys before every update-topology/deploy/save POST; dropAllKeys on controller import; in-flight mode-flip guard in topologyStore.compile() drops a result if mode flipped to controller mid-request. (2) secret-canvas flush at every gate via the single clearServerCanvasAtGate predicate (logout, checkSession session-loss x2, switchToLocal) — backs up dirty work first. (3) keystone status is SERVER-authoritative (serverOperatorPinned from GET /operator-credential), the browser-local operatorCredential* cache must NOT decide 'enrolled' (a cleared browser would falsely read 'Not enrolled' and invite a fleet-stranding re-pin); re-pin gated behind pendingKeystoneRotate + explicit rotate ack. (4) ctlURL rejects non-absolute/non-http(s) baseURL so the operator Bearer token can't be sent to the panel's own origin. 

The FE deliberately MIRRORS backend logic in pure helpers (healCollidingPins<->normalize, deriveUpdateState<->selfupdate, omitempty arrays<->json tags, webauthn bytes<->trustlist) — any backend model/tag/marker change is a silent cross-boundary break here. base64StdToBytes (controllerStore) pairs with Go base64.StdEncoding on trustlist_json specifically (footgun documented at controllerStore ~507): a Go switch to base64url silently breaks signing. 

Keystone deploy flow lives in controllerStore.deploy(): updateTopology -> stage -> if staged: GET /trustlist (null=keystone off -> promote directly; non-null -> signManifest with pinned cred -> POST /trustlist-signature -> promote) -> post-deploy reconcile (re-GET, mergeServerAllocations or full hydrate by sameIdSet). 

Component layer (~50 files under components/) is thin/presentational, organized by feature folder (canvas/ design/ deploy/ shell/ pages/ auth/ audit/ nodes/ domains/ layout/); nearly all read via the 3 zustand hooks; only the 5 deploy cards noted in smells reach the client directly. Pages are route bodies; Shell is the persistent layout with the controller login gate.

smells: DUAL render packages persist: internal/render (shared key-prep + render.All entry; CJK doc-comment says it was created to kill cmd/compiler's old degenerate FAKE_PRIVKEY/no-wg0 reimplementation, audit topic T6) AND internal/renderer (the text/template renderers). Both legitimately exist but the render/renderer name pair is a real readability trap for any newcomer and was flagged in the prompt — confirmed: render orchestrates, renderer emits.

ZERO tests on cmd/ — none of the three CLI mains (cmd/server/main.go mode-gate + graceful-shutdown, operator.go password/create-operator, cmd/agent/main.go 603-line daemon loop + self-update phase sequencing) has a *_test.go. The agent daemon loop / reconcile-phase ordering (the brick-risk surface) is exercised only indirectly via internal/agent unit tests, not at the main package.

NO frontend test runner at all: frontend/package.json has no test script, no vitest/jest, no *.test/*.spec files; CI 'frontend' job is lint+build only. The Zustand store / custody dropAllKeys / TS-mirror-of-Go-model has no automated test gate.

release.yml DUPLICATES ci.yml's gate logic by hand (gofmt-omitted variant: release re-runs vet+test+frontend-lint but skips the gofmt-drift check ci.yml runs) — two copies of the gate that can drift; the release gate is a strict subset, not identical.

internal/regression is a single 566-line file holding 9 tests (god-test-file by convention, though deliberate and self-documented as load-bearing non-release). It is the ONLY package whose name is a test-suite rather than a component — a downstream investigator must know it ships nothing.

Test mass is concentrated and uneven: internal/api (27 test files) + internal/controller (19) hold ~42% of the 109 test files; controller/store_compat_test.go (1441) and api/controller_http_test.go (1193) are >800-line god-test-files. Test LOC (26,792) EXCEEDS internal src LOC (22,323) ~1.2:1.
dev.sh uses `go run ./cmd/server/` (recompile-per-start) and lsof-based port-kill belt-and-suspenders — fragile if lsof absent; not a CI concern but a local-dev footgun.

scripts/deploy.sh (release-asset downloader) and the renderer-emitted per-node install.sh are two different 'deploy' artifacts with overlapping names; deploy.sh hardcodes OWNER=kunori-kiku/REPO defaults that must stay in sync with the actual GitHub repo path.
region_maps.notes: Coverage map: EVERY internal package that has source also has at least one *_test.go (no untested internal pkg). 109 Go test files, ~26.8k test LOC. The gaps are all OUTSIDE internal: (1) cmd/* mains untested, (2) frontend has no automated tests. CI (ci.yml) gates Go via gofmt-drift + `go vet ./...` + `go test ./...` (so internal/regression runs in CI like any package) and frontend via npm ci/lint/build; both run on PR and push-to-main. Release (release.yml) re-gates on v* tags then matrix-builds 7 targets (linux amd64/arm64/386/armv7, windows amd64/arm64/386) of all 3 binaries with the -X main.BuildVersion stamp, packages bundles + standalone signed-sidecar agent binaries, cuts the GitHub release. docker.yml builds the CONTROLLER-only multi-arch image (agent is host-installed, never containerized). Build determinism note for the air-gap investigators: cmd/compiler is documented (CJK comments + D-code refs D4/D6/D27-29/D59) to produce a BYTE-IDENTICAL bundle to the API path because both now route through render.All/render.GenerateKeys (the shared layer) — that equivalence is asserted in internal/render/equivalence_test.go. The single external-dep keygen (wgtypes) is pulled in by cmd/compiler->render and by internal/regression directly; it is the only WASM/browser-portability blocker and lives entirely below cmd/. go.sum is only 10 lines (minimal dependency surface, consistent with the stdlib-only design). The regression suite is _test.go-only (package regression, never linked into a release binary) and is treated as load-bearing: a failure there is a bug to fix, not suppress (per its own doc comment and MEMORY.md). All file paths cited are repo-relative under /home/kunorikiku/source/yet-another-overlay-generator/.

region_maps.notes: Direction of truth: STATUS.md + CHANGELOG.md are CURRENT (regenerated 2026-06-17, through v2.0.0-beta.7). The nested docs/spec/ is mostly current for the controller subsystem (controller/*.md are detailed and carry dated retractions), but two spec files lag the code: repository-structure.md (badly stale tree) and the README.md index lines that summarize the retracted mTLS model. The end-user wiki.md/wiki-zh.md are knowingly stale (air-gap-only). docs/audit/ and docs/design/ are HISTORICAL by design (pinned dates/commits) -- do not treat as current contracts. NAMING note for cross-region investigators: the spec/code distinguish internal/render (render.go entry point + artifacts_json + signing/custody tests) from internal/renderer (wireguard/babel/script/deploy/sysctl); the stale repository-structure.md only knows internal/renderer, so anyone using that doc to navigate will miss internal/render entirely. The audit dossier (docs/audit/2026-06-audit-dossier.md) is a useful index of the 14 historical root-cause themes (T1 port/endpoint ownership being the headline) and maps each theme to its intended spec home -- good orientation for why certain contracts (edge.md port ownership, peer-derivation.md resolution rule) read the way they do. No emoji, no secrets encountered in this region.
